question: Is the statement supported by the evidence?
answer: Answer — short conclusion - Likely connected: moderate-to-high probability that the three outages are related to weekend maintenance activity that touched perimeter (WAF/TLS), identity (ADFS/federation/cert), and server/OS layers. The pattern in the incident records points to a common failure domain in the identity/TLS layer (changes to WAF/templates, TLS/cipher and ADFS/federation settings) with an additional independent OS/restore failure for the SAP print servers that could explain the ERP delivery note symptom.

question: Is the statement supported by the evidence?
answer: . What the records show (evidence) - Customer portal 502 (external "Bad Gateway") was caused by a weekend security patch / profile reload on the perimeter publishing device which applied a Default Strict profile and removed the Portal App Template header rewrite (X Forwarded Proto); disabling TLS inspection restored access and re enabling it reproduced the 502 until the template/rewrites were restored

Kind regards Consulting Sample Company REPORT 13: # KF40 Portal Publication (portal.kf40.ch) — WAF/Reverse-Proxy and Firewall Operational Community This community centers on the perimeter publishing stack (WAF / Reverse Proxy) that publishes the Customer Company 40 portal (portal.kf40.ch) and the network/firewall estate that supports user connectivity (including Teams media). A high‑priority support ticket (TI-1594408) documented an external 502 Bad Gateway outage for portal.kf40.ch while internal access remained functional. Troubleshooting showed the backend application servers and load balancer were healthy, TLS certificates were validated and not the root cause, and the outage correlated to a weekend security patch/change that reloaded a Default-Strict inspection/profile and removed a custom header rewrite rule needed to present X-Forwarded-Proto: https to the backend. Restoring the Portal-App-Template / custom header rewrite configuration per the Secure Web Publishing blueprint resolved external access. Separately, a Zurich site firewall hardening change restricted outbound UDP to ports 3478–3481 only, blocking the dynamic RTP port range and causing one‑way/no audio in Teams; a temporary port opening restored media flows and confirmed the root cause. The incident and artifacts highlight technical, process, and governance gaps around post‑patch template/profile validation, change control for perimeter devices, and runbooks for header/HTTP profile preservation during TLS inspection/termination. ## Root cause of portal 502: WAF TLS inspection plus missing/custom header rewrite rule (X-Forwarded-Proto) prevented the backend from seeing HTTPS. External requests to portal.kf40.ch returned 502 Bad Gateway while internal access remained functional; investigation showed the reverse proxy/WAF performs TLS termination/inspection and is expected to forward X-Forwarded-Proto: https to the backend, but that header was not present when inspection was active, causing the backend to misinterpret the request and return errors. This causal chain is documented in the incident artifacts linking the REVERSE PROXY / WAF to the APPLICATION SERVER and noting the missing CUSTOM HEADER REWRITE RULE and absent X-FORWARDED-PROTO header as drivers for the 502s [Data: Entities (302,312,319); Relationships (492,499,496)]. Restoring the header rewrite behavior per the Portal-App-Template/blueprint returned external functionality, confirming the header rewrite absence (not the backend or certificate validity) was the functional trigger for failures [Data: Entities (311,300); Relationships (568,499)]. ## Security patch/template reload replaced Portal-App-Template with Default-Strict, causing template/profile drift and header removal. A weekend security patch caused the reverse proxy/WAF to reload a default inspection/profile (Default-Strict) which replaced the custom PORTAL-APP-TEMPLATE and its documented HTTP/profile/header‑rewrite rules; the activated Default-Strict profile altered header-handling and removed or failed to preserve X-Forwarded headers, reproducing the 502 when TLS inspection was enabled [Data: Entities (310,311); Relationships (497,568,548)]. This shows configuration/template drift introduced by patching—where a device reset to a default profile overwrote application‑specific templates—rather than an application or certificate error, creating a clear process risk for perimeter devices that host per-application templates [Data: Relationships (497,562)]. ## Backend and load‑balancing layer were healthy—issue isolated to publishing/inspection layer. Load balancer health checks and internal tests marked the backend application server as healthy and reachable, indicating the backend process was not the primary failure point; monitoring and health checks were used to validate stability after the fix before ticket closure [Data: Entities (305,318,308); Relationships (504,505,513)]. These diagnostics materially constrained the fault domain to the perimeter (WAF/reverse proxy) and saved time by directing remediation to template/profile/header handling rather than application restarts alone [Data: Relationships (492,563)]. ## TLS certificate change was investigated and ruled out as the root cause. The customer reported a recently installed TLS certificate and helpdesk validated the certificate CN, SAN and chain; while the certificate change was temporally proximate to the outage, diagnostics showed certificate validity/chain issues were not the cause of the 502s—the publishing layer header behavior was [Data: Entities (306); Relationships (486)]. The certificate service and blueprint decision tree were consulted as part of the normal checklist, which helped rule TLS problems out and focus on WAF behavior [Data: Relationships (552,508)]. ## Teams media outage at Zurich caused by outbound firewall restriction blocking dynamic RTP ports. A separate but contemporaneous operational change at the Zurich site implemented a security-hardening outbound rule that permitted only UDP 3478–3481, blocking the dynamic RTP port range required for Teams media when Media Bypass is used. This change produced no audio for external calls; temporarily opening the wider UDP/RTP range restored audio immediately, confirming the firewall rule as the root cause [Data: Entities (303,442,443); Relationships (739,741,742,733)]. Firewall logs and change records tied the timing of the outbound restriction to the onset of audio issues, showing this was a local change management failure with direct impact to voice/media availability [Data: Relationships (738,739)]. ## Blueprint and operational docs exist but were not enforced by the post‑patch validation process. An authoritative Secure Web Publishing blueprint and versioned guidance (BLUEPRINT: SECURE WEB PUBLISHING – KF40 – VERSION 3.2 / WEB PUBLISHING BLUEPRINT) defines required behaviors: TLS termination at the WAF, required Portal-App-Template, and documented header rewrite rules and validation after patches. The team retrieved the blueprint from DMS to align remediation, indicating awareness of the standard; however, the blueprint's requirement to validate WAF profiles and preserve custom rewrite rules was not enforced automatically during the patch that reset profiles, producing configuration drift [Data: Entities (307,339,356); Relationships (508,512,562)]. This gap between blueprint and operational enforcement—where a profile reload caused a prohibited Default-Strict to be applied despite the blueprint forbidding it—exposes the environment to repeat regressions unless validation and change controls are hardened [Data: Relationships (548,497)]. ## Operational ownership, escalation path, and remediation were used correctly but could be formalized into runbooks and automated checks. The high-priority ticket TI-1594408 was owned initially by Employee 29 (helpdesk) who performed initial checks and escalated to BU Network & Security; BU Network & Security then took ownership and applied WAF/reverse proxy configuration changes to restore service, with monitoring confirming 24 hours stability before closure—showing correct functional escalation and remediation occurred [Data: Entities (295,298,299); Relationships (479,487,476)]. The artifacts show manual steps and handoffs (tests, template restores, monitoring) that resolved the incident, but the manual nature of these actions recommends formalizing a post‑patch WAF validation runbook, automated configuration drift detection, and explicit pre/post patch checklists to reduce mean‑time‑to‑repair and recurrence risk [Data: Relationships (479,513)].

REPORT 2: # TRAIN CE / ADFS Integration: CE Train Server, ADFS Client Registration, and Toolbox MFA This community centers on the TRAIN CE/CE Train server integration that relies on on‑premises ADFS for authentication. A newly registered ADFS client used by the Toolbox to orchestrate MFA had EnableJWT and RequestMFAFromClaimsProviders enabled in PROD, which caused ADFS to issue JWTs and to forward MFA to external claims providers; those changes broke non‑browser integrations and produced widespread login/connectivity failures across environments. The incident affected the Train CE server, involved remediation actions (repair installation, federation metadata updates, certificate removals), and required coordinated troubleshooting by internal support engineers, the Identity/Architecture team and an external vendor (Avanade). Secondary factors observed during remediation included stale device compliance from hybrid Azure AD‑joined devices and Conditional Access evaluations that contributed to access variability for some users. The investigation produced both an ADFS configuration documentation artifact and guidance in a broader Identity & Authentication blueprint to prevent recurrence and to inform escalation and monitoring practices. ## Root cause: misconfigured ADFS client registration (EnableJWT + RequestMFAFromClaimsProviders) on a new client The investigation identified a new client application whose ADFS client registration had EnableJWT and RequestMFAFromClaimsProviders set to true; this caused ADFS to issue JWTs and to delegate MFA to external claims providers, which the downstream Toolbox/Train integration did not expect and therefore failed to authenticate. The new client registration and the parameter choices are explicitly recorded as the root cause and were adjusted to match the TEST environment to resolve the failures [Data: Entities (249,248,240,241); Relationships (390,370)]. Recommendation: enforce a documented client‑registration review process that verifies token format and MFA delegation flags before promoting client registrations to PROD, and add a checklist item to the CI/CD or change request to confirm EnableJWT and RequestMFAFromClaimsProviders settings for non‑browser integrations. ## Production impact and remediation steps applied to the CE Train server The CE Train server (TRAIN CE) experienced connectivity and authentication failures across users and environments; remediation steps recorded include a repair installation of the CE Train server (02.12.24), federation metadata updates on ADFS, and removal/rotation of old federation certificates—followed by ADFS parameter changes that restored correct behaviour. These actions and the target system are captured in the incident notes and ticket timeline [Data: Entities (252,235,254,253); Relationships (405,409,410,367)]. Recommendation: codify the validated remediation sequence (repair, federation metadata verification, certificate refresh, client parameter alignment) into a runbook for TRAIN CE integrations so responders can execute a repeatable playbook during future authentication incidents. ## Stakeholder and escalation map: support engineers, identity/architecture, application owners and external vendor involvement Multiple named stakeholders participated in triage and remediation: Employee 21 acted as lead analyst/owner for related incidents and executed configuration changes and device sync operations; Employee 14 reported and coordinated the initial ticket; Employee 24 and Admin appear in the ticket transfer chain; Avanade participated as an external troubleshooting partner; and the affected tenant and application owners are Customer Company 11AG / 11CE TRAIN. The ticket and assignment flows show cross‑team responsibility and vendor engagement during the incident [Data: Entities (232,4,231,10,229,230); Relationships (353,361,391,17,365)]. Recommendation: publish a concise stakeholder contact matrix and an escalation path (first responder/System Engineer -> Identity/Architecture -> Application Owner -> Vendor) and include roles, expected SLAs, and preferred communication channels for TRAIN CE‑class authentication incidents. ## Conditional Access and device sync were contributing factors for some access failures but were not the primary root cause Conditional Access was evaluated and did not present an outright blocking rule for the affected sign‑ins; however, some hybrid Azure AD‑joined devices reported stale or 'unknown' compliance for 6–8 hours, causing the 'Require compliant device' CA policy to evaluate as not satisfied and producing access variability for certain users. Employee 21 triggered manual device synchronization as part of remediation to update device compliance state faster [Data: Entities (683,680,688,684); Relationships (1199,1201,1206,1189)]. Recommendation: implement monitoring/alerts for device‑sync latency and include device synchronization health in incident triage dashboards; where appropriate, create temporary CA policy exceptions documented in change control but tied to a limited remediation window. ## Logging, monitoring and documentation gaps identified and addressed with configuration documentation and blueprint guidance The post‑incident artifacts include an ADFS configuration documentation entry capturing the corrected client registration settings and the investigation details, authored/confirmed by Employee 21, and a separate Identity & Authentication blueprint that prescribes decision trees, token format guidance, and escalation routes. The blueprint highlights the need for correlated logging across ADFS, claims providers and Entra ID to diagnose delegated MFA and JWT/SAML token flows [Data: Entities (244,232,207,219,208); Relationships (364,317,330)]. Recommendation: ensure the ADFS configuration documentation is versioned in the configuration management repository and update monitoring/alerting to collect token issuance events, claims‑provider logs and correlation IDs to shorten future diagnosis time. ## Token format and non‑browser flow mismatch risk (JWT vs SAML) for applications Enabling JWT issuance via EnableJWT changes the token format emitted by ADFS (JWT/OIDC) and requires that consuming applications support and validate JWTs; the blueprint warns that non‑browser integrations and service accounts commonly fail when token formats change unexpectedly. The incident demonstrates this risk where the new client caused JWT issuance that the Train integration could not process [Data: Entities (240,242,215,216); Relationships (379,324,334)]. Recommendation: require application owners to declare supported token formats in application inventories and gate any client registration change that switches token formats with explicit owner approval and a test plan validating non‑browser flows. ## Process and governance weakness: PROD‑only client registration change and insufficient pre‑deployment verification Investigators initially considered and briefly excluded ADFS because only PROD had been modified over the weekend; the error reproduced across environments, and only later was the misconfigured new client identified as the root cause—indicating a gap in change control or rollout validation for client registrations in PROD. The ticket timeline and decision notes record that the misconfigured registration was corrected to match TEST [Data: Entities (21,249,251); Relationships (65,228,390)]. Recommendation: implement formal change controls for ADFS client registrations (including peer review and a rollback plan), require a documented mapping between TEST and PROD registration parameters, and add pre‑promotion automated checks that detect risky flags like EnableJWT and RequestMFAFromClaimsProviders in non‑browser client contexts. ## Operationalizing the Decision Tree and runbook: where to escalate and what telemetry to collect The Identity & Authentication blueprint includes a decision tree for authentication issues (question flow for browser vs non‑browser, token type checks, MFA delegation checks and escalation criteria). The incident suggests operationalizing that decision tree into an incident runbook covering when to escalate to the Identity/Architecture team, what logs to collect (ADFS token issuance, claims provider events, Entra ID sign‑in and sync logs), and what immediate mitigations to try (client parameter rollback, device sync, certificate checks) [Data: Entities (225,207,220,219); Relationships (322,348,330)]. Recommendation: convert the blueprint decision tree into a one‑page runbook with command snippets for log collection, prioritized diagnostics, and a checklist for safe client parameter changes to shorten mean‑time‑to‑resolution and reduce handoff friction.

REPORT 16: # SAP Printing Outage — VRINFRGERPRNT03/04 (TI-931293) The community centers on the SAP printing path where SAP Spool/Output Management delivers jobs to SAP Print Targets that map to dedicated Windows Printserver VMs (notably VRINFRGERPRNT03 and VRINFRGERPRNT04). A high‑priority production incident (TI-931293) occurred 17–19.07.24 when SAP-generated orders could not be printed, blocking deliveries; the immediate remediation sequence included spooler restarts, a VM restore to a pre-maintenance snapshot, and disabling/re-enabling printer network shares which ultimately cleared queued jobs. The incident produced blue screens (BSOD) on the restored VMs and a customer request to rollback Windows versioning, and it exposed gaps in post-update/restore validation for queue properties, publishing/sharing parameters and end‑to‑end test prints. Multiple support engineers and the infrastructure on‑call team coordinated the recovery; the organisation has an SAP printing standard that prescribes post-change validation but the event still recurred across both SAP printserver VMs, indicating a likely server-image or Windows-update related root cause that requires deeper OS/driver/VM-host investigation and tighter runbook controls. [Data: Entities (534, 153, 154, 155, 147); Relationships (896, 913, 908, 232)] ## Production-impacting outage: SAP print jobs blocked, business deliveries affected The incident was recorded as a high‑priority printing outage affecting SAP-originated orders and deliveries, indicating direct business impact and the need for fast remediation and coordinated operations response. The ticket TI-931293 documents the inability to print from SAP and the classification and remediation timeline across 17–19.07.24. [Data: Entities (534); Relationships (896)]. Support actions were escalated to infrastructure on‑call and multiple engineers were assigned to the case, showing cross-shift coordination and handover requirements for production restores and follow-up testing (information sent to early shift for next‑day validation). This highlights both the operational severity and that the incident required multi-role participation to resolve. [Data: Relationships (905, 908)]. ## Probable root‑cause chain: Windows update -> VM restore -> network share/queue publishing corruption Documents and timelines link a Windows update and subsequent VM restore to the onset of queue-sharing and printing failures: a Windows update was applied, the print-server VMs were restored to a prior snapshot, and after the restore the printer network shares and queue publishing were malfunctioning, causing jobs to remain stuck until shares were toggled. This sequence is central to the incident's root-cause hypothesis and was specifically recorded in the log entries. [Data: Entities (537, 523); Relationships (890, 232, 883, 891)]. Contact Person 11 explicitly requested a rollback of the Windows version on the print servers, reinforcing the operational suspicion that the Windows update/version was implicated; the infrastructure on‑call also documented this suspicion. The appearance of BSODs after the restore further suggests OS/driver/host-level instability that must be investigated beyond application-level spooler troubleshooting. [Data: Relationships (923, 911); Entities (542)]. ## Failure manifested in both SAP printserver VMs but not in follow‑me print infrastructure Both VRINFRGERPRNT03 and VRINFRGERPRNT04 exhibited identical failure patterns (inability to process SAP jobs, failed spooler restarts, and BSODs after restore), while other print servers supporting the follow‑me solution continued to print normally. This scoped the incident to the SAP-designated printserver VMs rather than a tenant-wide network or SAP-side-only problem. [Data: Entities (154, 155, 535); Relationships (913, 901, 887)]. Because two independent VMs in the same role failed similarly after the same maintenance/change sequence, a systemic image, driver set, or host-level change (or a common post-restore validation omission) is a likely common-mode cause that requires coordinated forensic analysis across both VM instances and their hypervisor/backup/restore processes. [Data: Relationships (890, 232)]. ## Operational runbook/validation gaps: post-update/restore checks were insufficient or incomplete An approved 'Print Services for SAP Output' standard exists and mandates post-update/restore validation steps (queue status, spooler health, publishing/sharing parameters and test prints). The incident narrative cites VM restore and Windows update as triggers requiring these checks, but the persistent failure and need to manually toggle network shares indicate either incomplete adherence to the standard or that the current validation checklist does not detect the specific failure mode observed. [Data: Entities (147, 161); Relationships (243, 246, 251)]. This demonstrates both the presence of governance (a standard) and a procedural gap: either the runbook was not fully executed during the restoration window or the runbook lacks items (e.g., explicit verification of network-share registration and directory publishing state after restore) that would have detected this issue before business impact. The standard should be revised to explicitly include steps that caught this failure in TI-931293. [Data: Relationships (251, 891)]. ## Troubleshooting sequence and stakeholder actions were appropriate but reveal handover and evidence-capture weaknesses Employee 23 performed early remediation actions (gpupdate /force on an affected workstation, spooler restart attempts, and remote troubleshooting) and then activated infrastructure on‑call; the infrastructure on‑call performed the VM restore with support from a technician (Mister X) and restoration contexts (Inter12203/Inter12204). Multiple engineers later investigated BSODs and log evidence. These documented steps show correct escalation and involvement of required roles. [Data: Entities (49, 538, 541, 539, 540); Relationships (74, 921, 908, 924, 925)]. However, the fact that BSODs and the network-share corruption occurred after the restore suggests that pre‑ and post-restore evidence capture (detailed event/log snapshots, hypervisor logs, driver versions) and controlled validation were either not available or not sufficient to prevent or immediately diagnose the failure — an evidence-capture checklist and mandatory pre-remediation snapshot of key logs should be formalised. [Data: Relationships (911, 890)]. ## Security and availability exposures from share toggling and manual fixes Administrators cleared stuck jobs and restored printing by disabling and then re-enabling the printer network shares on the affected servers. While effective in this incident, this manual share manipulation is an operationally risky fix if done without change control because it temporarily alters SMB/print sharing state and could impact visibility or cause job loss if not coordinated and audited. The logs show that the printer network share was the primary locus of the problem and the point of manual remediation. [Data: Relationships (883, 891); Entities (524)]. Recommendation: treat share toggles as controlled mitigation steps in runbooks with explicit rollback/validation and audit logging to prevent accidental exposure or data loss. Consider automating share/publishing verification as part of post-update validation to avoid manual toggles except under controlled emergency procedures. [Data: Relationships (251, 246)]. ## Recommended immediate and medium-term actions (prioritised) Immediate (P0/P1) actions: 1) Isolate a restored printserver VM to collect memory dumps, Windows event logs, spooler logs and hypervisor host logs for the restore window to determine BSOD causes; 2) if not already done, roll back to the previously known-good Windows image/version on a non-production test copy to reproduce the failure before applying tenant-wide rollbacks; 3) add mandatory post-restore checklist enforcement (validate queue properties, publishing/sharing flags, directory publishing and run an SAP-originated test print) before returning servers to production. These map to items recorded in the standard and the ticket actions. [Data: Entities (147, 523, 537); Relationships (243, 246, 923)]. Medium-term actions: 1) Augment the 'Print Services for SAP Output' standard with explicit post-restore checks for network-share registration and add automated probes that verify queue visibility from SAP and a test workstation; 2) implement a pre/post-restore automated verification job that runs spooler/queue/publishing checks and preserves logs for forensics; 3) perform a controlled update/rollback plan for Windows versions across SAP printserver VMs (staged on a test VM, with clear rollback criteria). These changes will reduce recurrence and shorten mean time to resolution for similar future incidents. [Data: Entities (147, 161, 523); Relationships (890, 891)]. ## Communication, ownership and escalation matrix to formalise The incident involved CRM/web ticket logging, a primary early responder (Employee 23), escalation to infrastructure on‑call, handover to early shift for validation, and involvement of additional engineers (Employees 19 and 20) for BSOD and SAP-side checks. This shows a functioning ad-hoc escalation chain but also highlights the need for a formal matrix documenting responsibilities for: immediate remediation (spooler/queue checks), VM restore/host interactions, OS-level diagnostics, SAP-side validation, and customer communication/approvals. [Data: Entities (49, 516, 517, 538, 543); Relationships (74, 921, 905, 879, 906)]. Create an escalation runbook that lists required evidence to collect at each handover (logs, timestamps, snapshots), decision criteria for rollback vs. rebuild, and a named on-call roster to reduce ambiguity in future incidents. Ensure the runbook maps to the approved standard and is practiced in tabletop exercises. [Data: Entities (147); Relationships (905)].

The incident corpus documents exactly these component failures in different tickets.

The relationships document the dependency between AOS servers, users and TLS policy changes, and record that AOS restarts were necessary after cipher adjustments [Data: Entities (19,483,487); Relationships (796,798,36)]. IIS servers are also the target of IISCRYPTO changes and therefore form the TLS termination layer that directly affects client report access; the record linking IISCRYPTO to IIS servers and to the best-practice cipher-suite configuration confirms they are part of the change domain that produced the outage [Data: Entities (26,18,20); Relationships (35,34)]. ## TLS / cipher-suite configuration and Windows security updates are a plausible root-cause vector and must be part of diagnostics The dataset explicitly identifies TLS/cipher-suite configuration as a primary decision-tree item for multi-environment report failures and calls out that Windows security updates can alter available crypto libraries or TLS behavior, impacting negotiation and compatibility [Data: Entities (480,488); Relationships (803)]. Operationally this means any change to the TLS configuration (cipher suites or minimum TLS versions) or a cumulative OS/security patch may introduce incompatibilities between client/report-designer, IIS, AOS and backend services; the incident remediation (activating best-practice ciphers and restarting servers) and the diagnostic checklist in the records both point to these items as the first-line troubleshooting steps [Data: Entities (0,20,480,488); Relationships (9,803,36)]. ## The incident shows cross-regional recurrence — internal Jira ICO261-183 reports the same error An internal JIRA ticket (ICO261-183) opened by another internal team (Mexico) reported the same error, and the portal notes for TI-722511 reference that JIRA ticket, indicating the problem was observed outside a single operating region and should be treated as a global/tenant-level issue requiring coordinated remediation and communication [Data: Entities (17); Relationships (33); Entities (0); Relationships (0)]. This cross-reference implies the root cause is likely systemic (configuration or policy across environments) rather than an isolated host-level fault, elevating the need for a deployment-wide review of TLS/cipher policies, synchronized change windows, and shared runbooks to avoid reoccurrence [Data: Entities (17,0); Relationships (33,9)]. ## Consulting company acted as primary triage/coordination but operational ownership and escalation paths are fragmented Portal no-reply entries and the ticket relationships identify a consulting organization performing triage, posting portal updates and coordinating remediation activities for TI-722511, which indicates third-party involvement in operational execution [Data: Entities (24); Relationships (16)]. While external support can accelerate remediation, the records show multiple actors (customer contact, consulting company, internal teams, and referenced JIRA) are involved; this multiplicity increases coordination overhead and suggests the need to codify explicit escalation and handoff steps (who is authorized to change TLS settings, who validates post-change testing, and who owns cross-region communication) to reduce confusion during future incidents [Data: Entities (2,24,17); Relationships (1,16,33)]. ## The ticket closure documents a fix but there is limited explicit trace of pre- and post-change testing and rollback procedures Closure text states best-practice cipher suites were activated with IISCRYPTO and servers restarted, and this outcome is associated with resolution of the ticket, but the dataset does not include detailed pre-change compatibility testing results, a staged rollout record, or an explicit rollback plan linked to the change [Data: Entities (0,18,20); Relationships (9,34)]. For production TLS changes that can break client compatibility, the absence of documented staged testing or rollback guidance in the ticket and relationships represents an operational risk — future changes should include mandatory compatibility validation (Report Designer and browser tests across supported clients), versioned configuration snapshots, and an approved rollback procedure recorded in the change ticket before applying IISCRYPTO or similar tooling [Data: Entities (0,480,18); Relationships (9,34,35)]. ## Recommended immediate actions: formalize TLS change control, add AOS/IIS restart playbook, and update monitoring/alerts Given the documented remediation and dependencies, the highest-priority operational actions are: 1) formalize a TLS/cipher-suite change control process that enforces synchronized configuration across UAT and PROD and requires pre-change validation; 2) add a concise runbook for TLS changes that includes steps to use IISCRYPTO, how and when to restart IIS and AOS servers, and post-change verification for report access; and 3) tune monitoring/alerts to detect report designer failures, unknown report-owner symptoms, and TLS negotiation failures so teams can detect regressions quickly. These recommendations are directly derived from the incident facts that link TLS configuration, IISCRYPTO use, and server restarts to resolution [Data: Entities (0,18,20,19,26,480); Relationships (9,34,36,35,796)]. Implementing these controls will reduce time-to-detect and time-to-recover for similar incidents and make cross-team communication and escalation more predictable. ## Governance and long-term mitigation: standardize TLS baselines, document supported client profiles, and maintain a cross-region configuration registry The incident demonstrates the need for organizational governance around TLS baselines (approved cipher suites and minimum TLS versions) and explicit documentation of supported client versions for the Report Designer and browser clients. Records show the problem manifested across regions and environments and was tied to cipher-suite changes, reinforcing that a single source of truth for TLS configuration and supported client compatibility is required [Data: Entities (480,483); Relationships (803,796,36)]. A practical control is a configuration registry (or CMDB extension) that records TLS/cipher settings per environment and a pre-deployment verification checklist that gates any change; combine this with scheduled compatibility testing against a matrix of client versions to avoid breaking production users when adopting "best-practice" cryptographic settings that may remove older ciphers still required by legacy clients [Data: Entities (480,483); Relationships (36,796)]. ## Communication and escalation runbook: define stakeholder matrix and cross-reference internal tracking systems The records link the ticket to named customer contacts (Contact Person 7, Regina Jagsch), a consulting company, and an internal JIRA that reported the same error, indicating multiple stakeholders and tracking systems were in use during the incident [Data: Entities (2,11,24,17); Relationships (1,10,16,33)]. To streamline future incidents, create a short escalation runbook that maps roles (customer contact, platform-owner, consulting SPOC, security owner), primary communication channels, and which external/internal ticketing system must be updated (portal ticket and JIRA). Include templates for status updates and a requirement to escalate to platform engineering when TLS/cipher changes are proposed, ensuring a single coordinated change window and clear ownership for rollback and postmortem actions [Data: Entities (0,2,11,24,17); Relationships (1,10,16,33)].

question: Is the statement supported by the evidence?
answer: . - Path B (multiple related but partly independent failures): perimeter/WAF template reload produced portal 502s; ADFS client parameter change produced the authentication/integration failures; the SAP print servers suffered an OS/VM update+restore regression that produced printing/spooler faults. These can be concurrent if the same maintenance window applied different changes to different systems — the records show weekend changes across perimeter, identity and VM patch/restore activity, though not all change ticket metadata is present.

Closure description: Parameters adjusted on ADFS as in the TEST environment. 18.12.24 00:34 Re: TRAIN-CE/FO integration / MFA Dear Employee 7, During the joint analysis of the issue, we identified that two parameters were not correctly set during the ADFS client registration. In the new client application used specifically for the MFA request of the Toolbox on the Train system, the following parameters were set to true: EnableJWT RequestMFAFromClaimsProviders EnableJWT activates the issuance of JSON Web Tokens in the authentication process. If enabled, ADFS issues JWTs instead of other token formats such as SAML. These tokens are evaluated by the target application. If the target system does not support JWTs or cannot validate their signature, authentication fails. RequestMFAFromClaimsProviders forwards MFA requirements to configured claims providers. This delegates MFA execution to external providers and increases complexity in authentication flow, logging, and auditing. The combination of these parameters can lead to login failures if the target system is not designed to handle JWTs or external MFA claims. We have documented the configuration to avoid future issues. From our perspective, no further problems are expected after adjusting the parameters. If MFA works stably and the Train environment is reachable again, the login topic can be considered resolved. Kind regards Consulting Sample Company REPORT 13: # KF40 Portal Publication (portal.kf40.ch) — WAF/Reverse-Proxy and Firewall Operational Community This community centers on the perimeter publishing stack (WAF / Reverse Proxy) that publishes the Customer Company 40 portal (portal.kf40.ch) and the network/firewall estate that supports user connectivity (including Teams media). A high‑priority support ticket (TI-1594408) documented an external 502 Bad Gateway outage for portal.kf40.ch while internal access remained functional. Troubleshooting showed the backend application servers and load balancer were healthy, TLS certificates were validated and not the root cause, and the outage correlated to a weekend security patch/change that reloaded a Default-Strict inspection/profile and removed a custom header rewrite rule needed to present X-Forwarded-Proto: https to the backend. Restoring the Portal-App-Template / custom header rewrite configuration per the Secure Web Publishing blueprint resolved external access. Separately, a Zurich site firewall hardening change restricted outbound UDP to ports 3478–3481 only, blocking the dynamic RTP port range and causing one‑way/no audio in Teams; a temporary port opening restored media flows and confirmed the root cause. The incident and artifacts highlight technical, process, and governance gaps around post‑patch template/profile validation, change control for perimeter devices, and runbooks for header/HTTP profile preservation during TLS inspection/termination. ## Root cause of portal 502: WAF TLS inspection plus missing/custom header rewrite rule (X-Forwarded-Proto) prevented the backend from seeing HTTPS. External requests to portal.kf40.ch returned 502 Bad Gateway while internal access remained functional; investigation showed the reverse proxy/WAF performs TLS termination/inspection and is expected to forward X-Forwarded-Proto: https to the backend, but that header was not present when inspection was active, causing the backend to misinterpret the request and return errors. This causal chain is documented in the incident artifacts linking the REVERSE PROXY / WAF to the APPLICATION SERVER and noting the missing CUSTOM HEADER REWRITE RULE and absent X-FORWARDED-PROTO header as drivers for the 502s [Data: Entities (302,312,319); Relationships (492,499,496)]. Restoring the header rewrite behavior per the Portal-App-Template/blueprint returned external functionality, confirming the header rewrite absence (not the backend or certificate validity) was the functional trigger for failures [Data: Entities (311,300); Relationships (568,499)]. ## Security patch/template reload replaced Portal-App-Template with Default-Strict, causing template/profile drift and header removal. A weekend security patch caused the reverse proxy/WAF to reload a default inspection/profile (Default-Strict) which replaced the custom PORTAL-APP-TEMPLATE and its documented HTTP/profile/header‑rewrite rules; the activated Default-Strict profile altered header-handling and removed or failed to preserve X-Forwarded headers, reproducing the 502 when TLS inspection was enabled [Data: Entities (310,311); Relationships (497,568,548)]. This shows configuration/template drift introduced by patching—where a device reset to a default profile overwrote application‑specific templates—rather than an application or certificate error, creating a clear process risk for perimeter devices that host per-application templates [Data: Relationships (497,562)]. ## Backend and load‑balancing layer were healthy—issue isolated to publishing/inspection layer. Load balancer health checks and internal tests marked the backend application server as healthy and reachable, indicating the backend process was not the primary failure point; monitoring and health checks were used to validate stability after the fix before ticket closure [Data: Entities (305,318,308); Relationships (504,505,513)]. These diagnostics materially constrained the fault domain to the perimeter (WAF/reverse proxy) and saved time by directing remediation to template/profile/header handling rather than application restarts alone [Data: Relationships (492,563)]. ## TLS certificate change was investigated and ruled out as the root cause. The customer reported a recently installed TLS certificate and helpdesk validated the certificate CN, SAN and chain; while the certificate change was temporally proximate to the outage, diagnostics showed certificate validity/chain issues were not the cause of the 502s—the publishing layer header behavior was [Data: Entities (306); Relationships (486)]. The certificate service and blueprint decision tree were consulted as part of the normal checklist, which helped rule TLS problems out and focus on WAF behavior [Data: Relationships (552,508)]. ## Teams media outage at Zurich caused by outbound firewall restriction blocking dynamic RTP ports. A separate but contemporaneous operational change at the Zurich site implemented a security-hardening outbound rule that permitted only UDP 3478–3481, blocking the dynamic RTP port range required for Teams media when Media Bypass is used. This change produced no audio for external calls; temporarily opening the wider UDP/RTP range restored audio immediately, confirming the firewall rule as the root cause [Data: Entities (303,442,443); Relationships (739,741,742,733)]. Firewall logs and change records tied the timing of the outbound restriction to the onset of audio issues, showing this was a local change management failure with direct impact to voice/media availability [Data: Relationships (738,739)]. ## Blueprint and operational docs exist but were not enforced by the post‑patch validation process. An authoritative Secure Web Publishing blueprint and versioned guidance (BLUEPRINT: SECURE WEB PUBLISHING – KF40 – VERSION 3.2 / WEB PUBLISHING BLUEPRINT) defines required behaviors: TLS termination at the WAF, required Portal-App-Template, and documented header rewrite rules and validation after patches. The team retrieved the blueprint from DMS to align remediation, indicating awareness of the standard; however, the blueprint's requirement to validate WAF profiles and preserve custom rewrite rules was not enforced automatically during the patch that reset profiles, producing configuration drift [Data: Entities (307,339,356); Relationships (508,512,562)]. This gap between blueprint and operational enforcement—where a profile reload caused a prohibited Default-Strict to be applied despite the blueprint forbidding it—exposes the environment to repeat regressions unless validation and change controls are hardened [Data: Relationships (548,497)]. ## Operational ownership, escalation path, and remediation were used correctly but could be formalized into runbooks and automated checks. The high-priority ticket TI-1594408 was owned initially by Employee 29 (helpdesk) who performed initial checks and escalated to BU Network & Security; BU Network & Security then took ownership and applied WAF/reverse proxy configuration changes to restore service, with monitoring confirming 24 hours stability before closure—showing correct functional escalation and remediation occurred [Data: Entities (295,298,299); Relationships (479,487,476)]. The artifacts show manual steps and handoffs (tests, template restores, monitoring) that resolved the incident, but the manual nature of these actions recommends formalizing a post‑patch WAF validation runbook, automated configuration drift detection, and explicit pre/post patch checklists to reduce mean‑time‑to‑repair and recurrence risk [Data: Relationships (479,513)].

If enabled, ADFS issues JWTs instead of other token formats such as SAML. These tokens are evaluated by the target application. REPORT 2: # TRAIN CE / ADFS Integration: CE Train Server, ADFS Client Registration, and Toolbox MFA This community centers on the TRAIN CE/CE Train server integration that relies on on‑premises ADFS for authentication. A newly registered ADFS client used by the Toolbox to orchestrate MFA had EnableJWT and RequestMFAFromClaimsProviders enabled in PROD, which caused ADFS to issue JWTs and to forward MFA to external claims providers; those changes broke non‑browser integrations and produced widespread login/connectivity failures across environments. The incident affected the Train CE server, involved remediation actions (repair installation, federation metadata updates, certificate removals), and required coordinated troubleshooting by internal support engineers, the Identity/Architecture team and an external vendor (Avanade). Secondary factors observed during remediation included stale device compliance from hybrid Azure AD‑joined devices and Conditional Access evaluations that contributed to access variability for some users. The investigation produced both an ADFS configuration documentation artifact and guidance in a broader Identity & Authentication blueprint to prevent recurrence and to inform escalation and monitoring practices. ## Root cause: misconfigured ADFS client registration (EnableJWT + RequestMFAFromClaimsProviders) on a new client The investigation identified a new client application whose ADFS client registration had EnableJWT and RequestMFAFromClaimsProviders set to true; this caused ADFS to issue JWTs and to delegate MFA to external claims providers, which the downstream Toolbox/Train integration did not expect and therefore failed to authenticate. The new client registration and the parameter choices are explicitly recorded as the root cause and were adjusted to match the TEST environment to resolve the failures [Data: Entities (249,248,240,241); Relationships (390,370)]. Recommendation: enforce a documented client‑registration review process that verifies token format and MFA delegation flags before promoting client registrations to PROD, and add a checklist item to the CI/CD or change request to confirm EnableJWT and RequestMFAFromClaimsProviders settings for non‑browser integrations. ## Production impact and remediation steps applied to the CE Train server The CE Train server (TRAIN CE) experienced connectivity and authentication failures across users and environments; remediation steps recorded include a repair installation of the CE Train server (02.12.24), federation metadata updates on ADFS, and removal/rotation of old federation certificates—followed by ADFS parameter changes that restored correct behaviour. These actions and the target system are captured in the incident notes and ticket timeline [Data: Entities (252,235,254,253); Relationships (405,409,410,367)]. Recommendation: codify the validated remediation sequence (repair, federation metadata verification, certificate refresh, client parameter alignment) into a runbook for TRAIN CE integrations so responders can execute a repeatable playbook during future authentication incidents. ## Stakeholder and escalation map: support engineers, identity/architecture, application owners and external vendor involvement Multiple named stakeholders participated in triage and remediation: Employee 21 acted as lead analyst/owner for related incidents and executed configuration changes and device sync operations; Employee 14 reported and coordinated the initial ticket; Employee 24 and Admin appear in the ticket transfer chain; Avanade participated as an external troubleshooting partner; and the affected tenant and application owners are Customer Company 11AG / 11CE TRAIN. The ticket and assignment flows show cross‑team responsibility and vendor engagement during the incident [Data: Entities (232,4,231,10,229,230); Relationships (353,361,391,17,365)]. Recommendation: publish a concise stakeholder contact matrix and an escalation path (first responder/System Engineer -> Identity/Architecture -> Application Owner -> Vendor) and include roles, expected SLAs, and preferred communication channels for TRAIN CE‑class authentication incidents. ## Conditional Access and device sync were contributing factors for some access failures but were not the primary root cause Conditional Access was evaluated and did not present an outright blocking rule for the affected sign‑ins; however, some hybrid Azure AD‑joined devices reported stale or 'unknown' compliance for 6–8 hours, causing the 'Require compliant device' CA policy to evaluate as not satisfied and producing access variability for certain users. Employee 21 triggered manual device synchronization as part of remediation to update device compliance state faster [Data: Entities (683,680,688,684); Relationships (1199,1201,1206,1189)]. Recommendation: implement monitoring/alerts for device‑sync latency and include device synchronization health in incident triage dashboards; where appropriate, create temporary CA policy exceptions documented in change control but tied to a limited remediation window. ## Logging, monitoring and documentation gaps identified and addressed with configuration documentation and blueprint guidance The post‑incident artifacts include an ADFS configuration documentation entry capturing the corrected client registration settings and the investigation details, authored/confirmed by Employee 21, and a separate Identity & Authentication blueprint that prescribes decision trees, token format guidance, and escalation routes. The blueprint highlights the need for correlated logging across ADFS, claims providers and Entra ID to diagnose delegated MFA and JWT/SAML token flows [Data: Entities (244,232,207,219,208); Relationships (364,317,330)]. Recommendation: ensure the ADFS configuration documentation is versioned in the configuration management repository and update monitoring/alerting to collect token issuance events, claims‑provider logs and correlation IDs to shorten future diagnosis time. ## Token format and non‑browser flow mismatch risk (JWT vs SAML) for applications Enabling JWT issuance via EnableJWT changes the token format emitted by ADFS (JWT/OIDC) and requires that consuming applications support and validate JWTs; the blueprint warns that non‑browser integrations and service accounts commonly fail when token formats change unexpectedly. The incident demonstrates this risk where the new client caused JWT issuance that the Train integration could not process [Data: Entities (240,242,215,216); Relationships (379,324,334)]. Recommendation: require application owners to declare supported token formats in application inventories and gate any client registration change that switches token formats with explicit owner approval and a test plan validating non‑browser flows. ## Process and governance weakness: PROD‑only client registration change and insufficient pre‑deployment verification Investigators initially considered and briefly excluded ADFS because only PROD had been modified over the weekend; the error reproduced across environments, and only later was the misconfigured new client identified as the root cause—indicating a gap in change control or rollout validation for client registrations in PROD. The ticket timeline and decision notes record that the misconfigured registration was corrected to match TEST [Data: Entities (21,249,251); Relationships (65,228,390)]. Recommendation: implement formal change controls for ADFS client registrations (including peer review and a rollback plan), require a documented mapping between TEST and PROD registration parameters, and add pre‑promotion automated checks that detect risky flags like EnableJWT and RequestMFAFromClaimsProviders in non‑browser client contexts. ## Operationalizing the Decision Tree and runbook: where to escalate and what telemetry to collect The Identity & Authentication blueprint includes a decision tree for authentication issues (question flow for browser vs non‑browser, token type checks, MFA delegation checks and escalation criteria). The incident suggests operationalizing that decision tree into an incident runbook covering when to escalate to the Identity/Architecture team, what logs to collect (ADFS token issuance, claims provider events, Entra ID sign‑in and sync logs), and what immediate mitigations to try (client parameter rollback, device sync, certificate checks) [Data: Entities (225,207,220,219); Relationships (322,348,330)]. Recommendation: convert the blueprint decision tree into a one‑page runbook with command snippets for log collection, prioritized diagnostics, and a checklist for safe client parameter changes to shorten mean‑time‑to‑resolution and reduce handoff friction.

context: SOURCE 40: ﻿general info & description Active Directory Domain Controller & Microsoft Defender for Identity Standard Version 1.0 Date: 10.10.2024 Status: Approved This document describes the architecture, security requirements and operational standards for Domain Controllers with installed Microsoft Defender for Identity Sensor within Customer Company 10. The objective is: • Stable monitoring of Active Directory activities • Proper functionality of the Defender for Identity sensors • Ensuring the necessary network and firewall permissions • Standardized configuration across all environments (On-Prem and Azure DCs) Microsoft Defender for Identity sensors run directly on the Domain Controllers and analyze: • Authentication processes • Kerberos and NTLM traffic • LDAP communication • Replication behavior Misconfigurations typically result in: • Sensor status “unhealthy” • Connection errors to the Defender Cloud Service • Missing security alerts • Recurring health alerts design Architecture overview Domain Controller (On-Prem / Azure) → Defender for Identity Sensor → Windows Firewall → Corporate Firewall → Microsoft Defender Cloud Service Components 1. Domain Controller Hosts Active Directory Services and the Defender Sensor 2. Defender for Identity Sensor Captures and analyzes authentication and network traffic 3. Firewall Layer o Windows Firewall on DC o Perimeter firewall o Azure NSG (for Azure DCs) 4. Defender Cloud Service Receives telemetry data for security analysis Network and port requirements Domain Controllers with sensor require: • Outbound HTTPS connection (TCP 443) • DNS resolution of external Microsoft endpoints • Access to specific Defender Service URLs • Internal AD ports (LDAP, Kerberos, RPC) unchanged Blocked or filtered connections result in “unhealthy” status. Operational principles • Sensor installation must be performed consistently on all DCs • Firewall rules must be configured according to Microsoft recommendations • Changes to network segments must be reviewed • After agent reinstallation, health status must be monitored • Parallel installation of multiple sensors per DC is not permitted decision tree & use cases Initial question Is only a single Domain Controller affected or multiple? A) Only one DC affected 1. Is the Defender Sensor service running? 2. Is outbound HTTPS connectivity available (Port 443)? 3. Is DNS resolution to Microsoft Defender endpoints successful? 4. Check event logs (Sensor / network errors) 5. If installation is inconsistent → remove agent and reinstall B) Multiple DCs affected 1. Has a firewall or NSG rule been changed? 2. Has a network segment been redefined? 3. Has a proxy or TLS policy been modified? 4. Is general internet connectivity available? C) Sensor repeatedly switches from “healthy” to “unhealthy” 1. Check for temporary network interruption 2. Check TLS or certificate issues 3. Check proxy authentication 4. Check agent version D) Issue persists after reinstallation 1. Verify that firewall rules are complete 2. Check whether Azure NSG blocks outbound connections 3. Check Defender Service Health Portal 4. Is internal security software blocking telemetry? REPORT 4: # KF10.com Domain Controllers and Microsoft Defender for Identity Sensors The community centers on Active Directory Domain Controllers (on‑prem and Azure) with Microsoft Defender for Identity sensors installed; sensors run on the DC hosts, send telemetry to the Defender Cloud Service over outbound HTTPS (TCP 443), and depend on a chain of network and host controls (Windows Firewall, perimeter firewall, Azure NSG), TLS/certificate validity, proxy configuration and local security software. The environment is governed by a written architecture/operational standard and a decision tree/troubleshooting flow that prescribes checks for service state, connectivity (DNS/443), event logs, TLS/proxy, and reinstall/monitor steps. Disruptions to any of these dependencies cause sensors to report “unhealthy”, reduce or eliminate health alerts in the cloud, and create blind spots in AD authentication/replication monitoring. [Data: Entities (696,697,698,702,699); Relationships (1231,1235,1255,1253,1258)] ## Domain Controllers and Defender sensors are tightly coupled and form the core monitoring plane Defender sensors run directly on Domain Controllers and capture authentication (Kerberos/NTLM), LDAP and replication telemetry; DC health and sensor behavior are tightly coupled, making the DCs and sensors a single operational surface that must be maintained together. The standard and operational guidance explicitly treat DCs with sensors as the primary subject for architecture and operations. This coupling means DC outages, host‑level changes, or misconfigured sensors directly degrade AD security visibility. [Data: Entities (696,697), Relationships (1231,1245,1246,1228)] ## Outbound HTTPS (TCP 443) to the Defender Cloud is a single critical network dependency Sensors rely on outbound HTTPS to send telemetry to the Defender Cloud Service; blocking or filtering TCP 443 prevents telemetry delivery and causes sensors to be marked unhealthy. The relationship between sensors and the cloud service is explicit and the document lists outbound HTTPS as a mandatory requirement. Network edge/firewall rules and Azure NSGs are singled out as controls that can interrupt this egress, which means perimeter and cloud egress policies are a primary root cause class for multi‑DC connectivity failures. [Data: Entities (702,698), Relationships (1235,1255,1258)] ## Host and perimeter network controls (Windows Firewall, perimeter firewall, Azure NSG) are frequent gating factors On‑host Windows Firewall settings on on‑prem DCs, perimeter firewall rules at the network edge, and Azure NSG rules for cloud DCs are all called out as required to permit sensor outbound connections and internal AD ports. Misconfiguration anywhere in this chain can simultaneously block telemetry and break AD protocols (LDAP/Kerberos/RPC), producing both service impact and monitoring blind spots. This indicates a need for coordinated change control across host, network, and cloud teams when modifying firewall/NSG rules. [Data: Entities (699,700,701,703), Relationships (1253,1255,1258,1261)] ## TLS/certificate problems, proxy authentication, and internal security software are persistent secondary failure modes The document lists TLS/certificate issues as an item that can prevent secure HTTPS between sensors and Microsoft endpoints; it also calls out proxy configuration/authentication and internal security software (host or inline appliances) as causes that can block or interfere with sensor egress. TLS mismatches or policy changes can cause failed egress despite port availability, while proxies or local security controls may require explicit exceptions or credentials for the sensors. These are common categories of intermittent or oscillating healthy/unhealthy sensor reports and should be included in root‑cause workflows. [Data: Entities (705,704,714), Relationships (1242,1241,1249,1260)] ## Sensor operational state, event logs and the decision tree are the primary diagnostic artefacts Operational checks emphasize verifying the sensor service state (running/reinstall), reviewing local sensor and system event logs, and following the decision tree that differentiates single‑DC vs multi‑DC issues. The decision tree maps to concrete troubleshooting steps: service state, outbound connectivity/DNS, proxy/TLS checks, logs and reinstall guidance; the Defender Service Health Portal is recommended to separate tenant/local problems from Microsoft service outages. These diagnostic artefacts should be centrally collected and accessible to on‑call teams for faster triage. [Data: Entities (708,712,709,706), Relationships (1243,1247,1264,1263)] ## Unhealthy sensors lead to missing or delayed health alerts and create security visibility gaps Sensor telemetry feeds the health/alerting pipeline in the Defender Cloud Service; when sensors are unhealthy or cannot reach the cloud, health alerts are absent or delayed, reducing detection capability for authentication anomalies and replication issues. This is a direct security and compliance exposure because AD authentication telemetry is critical for detecting lateral movement and credential misuse in the environment. The linkage between sensor connectivity and alert production is explicitly documented. [Data: Entities (713,698), Relationships (1248,1235)] ## Standards and the architecture document exist but network/topology change control is an elevated risk area An architecture and operational standard (Active Directory Domain Controller & Microsoft Defender for Identity Standard V1.0) and a decision tree/troubleshooting flow are documented, prescribing required ports and operational checks. However, the document specifically requires review of network segment/topology changes because such changes can alter the effective scope of perimeter firewall rules and NSGs, potentially blocking sensor traffic. This identifies network/topology change management as a governance gap to enforce when changes are planned. [Data: Entities (695,709,707), Relationships (1228,1256)] ## Priority remediation and operational recommendations align directly to documented checks Immediate high‑value actions are to: (1) verify outbound HTTPS (TCP 443) egress from affected DCs (host and perimeter/NSG), (2) confirm the sensor service is running and review sensor/system event logs, (3) validate TLS/certificate validity and proxy authentication/whitelisting, and (4) consult the Defender Service Health Portal to rule out cloud service issues. These steps are the same checks defined in the decision tree and should be codified into an escalation runbook and enforced via change control for firewall/NSG and topology changes to avoid recurrence. Implementing centralized log collection and automated health monitoring for sensor state would reduce MTTR. [Data: Entities (702,708,705,709), Relationships (1255,1243,1242,1263)]

REPORT 16: # SAP Printing Outage — VRINFRGERPRNT03/04 (TI-931293) The community centers on the SAP printing path where SAP Spool/Output Management delivers jobs to SAP Print Targets that map to dedicated Windows Printserver VMs (notably VRINFRGERPRNT03 and VRINFRGERPRNT04). A high‑priority production incident (TI-931293) occurred 17–19.07.24 when SAP-generated orders could not be printed, blocking deliveries; the immediate remediation sequence included spooler restarts, a VM restore to a pre-maintenance snapshot, and disabling/re-enabling printer network shares which ultimately cleared queued jobs. The incident produced blue screens (BSOD) on the restored VMs and a customer request to rollback Windows versioning, and it exposed gaps in post-update/restore validation for queue properties, publishing/sharing parameters and end‑to‑end test prints. Multiple support engineers and the infrastructure on‑call team coordinated the recovery; the organisation has an SAP printing standard that prescribes post-change validation but the event still recurred across both SAP printserver VMs, indicating a likely server-image or Windows-update related root cause that requires deeper OS/driver/VM-host investigation and tighter runbook controls. [Data: Entities (534, 153, 154, 155, 147); Relationships (896, 913, 908, 232)] ## Production-impacting outage: SAP print jobs blocked, business deliveries affected The incident was recorded as a high‑priority printing outage affecting SAP-originated orders and deliveries, indicating direct business impact and the need for fast remediation and coordinated operations response. The ticket TI-931293 documents the inability to print from SAP and the classification and remediation timeline across 17–19.07.24. [Data: Entities (534); Relationships (896)]. Support actions were escalated to infrastructure on‑call and multiple engineers were assigned to the case, showing cross-shift coordination and handover requirements for production restores and follow-up testing (information sent to early shift for next‑day validation). This highlights both the operational severity and that the incident required multi-role participation to resolve. [Data: Relationships (905, 908)]. ## Probable root‑cause chain: Windows update -> VM restore -> network share/queue publishing corruption Documents and timelines link a Windows update and subsequent VM restore to the onset of queue-sharing and printing failures: a Windows update was applied, the print-server VMs were restored to a prior snapshot, and after the restore the printer network shares and queue publishing were malfunctioning, causing jobs to remain stuck until shares were toggled. This sequence is central to the incident's root-cause hypothesis and was specifically recorded in the log entries. [Data: Entities (537, 523); Relationships (890, 232, 883, 891)]. Contact Person 11 explicitly requested a rollback of the Windows version on the print servers, reinforcing the operational suspicion that the Windows update/version was implicated; the infrastructure on‑call also documented this suspicion. The appearance of BSODs after the restore further suggests OS/driver/host-level instability that must be investigated beyond application-level spooler troubleshooting. [Data: Relationships (923, 911); Entities (542)]. ## Failure manifested in both SAP printserver VMs but not in follow‑me print infrastructure Both VRINFRGERPRNT03 and VRINFRGERPRNT04 exhibited identical failure patterns (inability to process SAP jobs, failed spooler restarts, and BSODs after restore), while other print servers supporting the follow‑me solution continued to print normally. This scoped the incident to the SAP-designated printserver VMs rather than a tenant-wide network or SAP-side-only problem. [Data: Entities (154, 155, 535); Relationships (913, 901, 887)]. Because two independent VMs in the same role failed similarly after the same maintenance/change sequence, a systemic image, driver set, or host-level change (or a common post-restore validation omission) is a likely common-mode cause that requires coordinated forensic analysis across both VM instances and their hypervisor/backup/restore processes. [Data: Relationships (890, 232)]. ## Operational runbook/validation gaps: post-update/restore checks were insufficient or incomplete An approved 'Print Services for SAP Output' standard exists and mandates post-update/restore validation steps (queue status, spooler health, publishing/sharing parameters and test prints). The incident narrative cites VM restore and Windows update as triggers requiring these checks, but the persistent failure and need to manually toggle network shares indicate either incomplete adherence to the standard or that the current validation checklist does not detect the specific failure mode observed. [Data: Entities (147, 161); Relationships (243, 246, 251)]. This demonstrates both the presence of governance (a standard) and a procedural gap: either the runbook was not fully executed during the restoration window or the runbook lacks items (e.g., explicit verification of network-share registration and directory publishing state after restore) that would have detected this issue before business impact. The standard should be revised to explicitly include steps that caught this failure in TI-931293. [Data: Relationships (251, 891)]. ## Troubleshooting sequence and stakeholder actions were appropriate but reveal handover and evidence-capture weaknesses Employee 23 performed early remediation actions (gpupdate /force on an affected workstation, spooler restart attempts, and remote troubleshooting) and then activated infrastructure on‑call; the infrastructure on‑call performed the VM restore with support from a technician (Mister X) and restoration contexts (Inter12203/Inter12204). Multiple engineers later investigated BSODs and log evidence. These documented steps show correct escalation and involvement of required roles. [Data: Entities (49, 538, 541, 539, 540); Relationships (74, 921, 908, 924, 925)]. However, the fact that BSODs and the network-share corruption occurred after the restore suggests that pre‑ and post-restore evidence capture (detailed event/log snapshots, hypervisor logs, driver versions) and controlled validation were either not available or not sufficient to prevent or immediately diagnose the failure — an evidence-capture checklist and mandatory pre-remediation snapshot of key logs should be formalised. [Data: Relationships (911, 890)]. ## Security and availability exposures from share toggling and manual fixes Administrators cleared stuck jobs and restored printing by disabling and then re-enabling the printer network shares on the affected servers. While effective in this incident, this manual share manipulation is an operationally risky fix if done without change control because it temporarily alters SMB/print sharing state and could impact visibility or cause job loss if not coordinated and audited. The logs show that the printer network share was the primary locus of the problem and the point of manual remediation. [Data: Relationships (883, 891); Entities (524)]. Recommendation: treat share toggles as controlled mitigation steps in runbooks with explicit rollback/validation and audit logging to prevent accidental exposure or data loss. Consider automating share/publishing verification as part of post-update validation to avoid manual toggles except under controlled emergency procedures. [Data: Relationships (251, 246)]. ## Recommended immediate and medium-term actions (prioritised) Immediate (P0/P1) actions: 1) Isolate a restored printserver VM to collect memory dumps, Windows event logs, spooler logs and hypervisor host logs for the restore window to determine BSOD causes; 2) if not already done, roll back to the previously known-good Windows image/version on a non-production test copy to reproduce the failure before applying tenant-wide rollbacks; 3) add mandatory post-restore checklist enforcement (validate queue properties, publishing/sharing flags, directory publishing and run an SAP-originated test print) before returning servers to production. These map to items recorded in the standard and the ticket actions. [Data: Entities (147, 523, 537); Relationships (243, 246, 923)]. Medium-term actions: 1) Augment the 'Print Services for SAP Output' standard with explicit post-restore checks for network-share registration and add automated probes that verify queue visibility from SAP and a test workstation; 2) implement a pre/post-restore automated verification job that runs spooler/queue/publishing checks and preserves logs for forensics; 3) perform a controlled update/rollback plan for Windows versions across SAP printserver VMs (staged on a test VM, with clear rollback criteria). These changes will reduce recurrence and shorten mean time to resolution for similar future incidents. [Data: Entities (147, 161, 523); Relationships (890, 891)]. ## Communication, ownership and escalation matrix to formalise The incident involved CRM/web ticket logging, a primary early responder (Employee 23), escalation to infrastructure on‑call, handover to early shift for validation, and involvement of additional engineers (Employees 19 and 20) for BSOD and SAP-side checks. This shows a functioning ad-hoc escalation chain but also highlights the need for a formal matrix documenting responsibilities for: immediate remediation (spooler/queue checks), VM restore/host interactions, OS-level diagnostics, SAP-side validation, and customer communication/approvals. [Data: Entities (49, 516, 517, 538, 543); Relationships (74, 921, 905, 879, 906)]. Create an escalation runbook that lists required evidence to collect at each handover (logs, timestamps, snapshots), decision criteria for rollback vs. rebuild, and a named on-call roster to reduce ambiguity in future incidents. Ensure the runbook maps to the approved standard and is practiced in tabletop exercises. [Data: Entities (147); Relationships (905)].

REPORT 141: # IIS Servers — TLS Cipher Hardening (IISCRYPTO & Best-practice Cipher Suites) This community centers on a security remediation that enabled best-practice TLS cipher suites on IIS web servers using the IISCRYPTO tool; the change (and associated server restarts) is documented as the resolution for ticket TI-722511.
