REPORT 56: # Management Reporting & Financial Reporting Infrastructure Standard (D365) — Customer Company 11 An approved operational and architecture standard (Management Reporting & Financial Reporting Infrastructure Standard V1.0) defines the design, security configuration and operational procedures for the Financial Reporting stack used in Customer Company 11's D365 Finance & Operations environments (UAT and PROD). The standard names a small set of high-impact components and dependencies: the Financial Reporting Data Mart (aggregated SQL store), the SQL backend that hosts metadata, an IIS/TLS layer that enforces cipher suites, service accounts used by reporting components, and operational actions such as Data Mart resets and decision-tree guided troubleshooting. The decision-tree/use-cases section maps observable symptoms (for example, reports showing authors as “unknown”) to concrete checks (service account connectivity, TLS/cipher-suite compatibility, SQL connectivity, AOS service, IIS configuration) and prescribes synchronous, environment-consistent changes (for cipher suites, AOS restarts, and Data Mart resets) to avoid broken flows across reporting components. The document is specifically authored for Customer Company 11 and is the authoritative reference tying troubleshooting, configuration management and recovery actions together. ## Standard document is the central governance artifact for reporting infrastructure The Management Reporting & Financial Reporting Infrastructure Standard V1.0 is the controlling architecture and operational standard for the Financial Reporting components in Customer Company 11's D365 environment and explicitly applies to UAT and PROD environments, making it the authoritative source for configuration and runbook behavior [Data: Entities (476); Relationships (778)]. Because the decision-tree and use-case sections are embedded in the same document, the standard both prescribes expected runtime configuration (TLS/cipher-suite rules, service-account behavior) and enumerates the troubleshooting workflow that engineers must follow when incidents occur, tying governance to operations [Data: Entities (476, 484); Relationships (784)]. ## Financial Reporting Data Mart is a single, critical dependency for report availability and correctness The Financial Reporting Data Mart is described as an aggregated SQL-based store relied upon by the Report Designer and Financial Reporting Service; its consistency is essential to correct report results and availability, which makes it a primary dependency in incident investigations [Data: Entities (478); Relationships (792)]. Operational guidance in the standard endorses a Data Mart reset as a remediation when data inconsistency is isolated to a single environment, indicating that the Data Mart is both a frequent root-cause locus and a recoverable subsystem when handled according to the runbook [Data: Entities (489); Relationships (795)]. ## SQL backend is the metadata authority and first-line troubleshooting target Report metadata and authorship information are stored and retrieved via the SQL backend/Data Mart; therefore SQL connectivity and database health directly affect metadata visibility and report rendering [Data: Entities (482, 485); Relationships (807)]. The decision-tree explicitly includes SQL connection checks as a primary step when reports or data are unavailable, making SQL health an early and repeatable check in incident triage workflows [Data: Entities (484, 482); Relationships (806)]. ## IIS/TLS layer and cipher-suite configuration are high-risk failure points for authentication and metadata issues An IIS-based TLS termination layer enforces TLS and cipher-suite settings for the reporting infrastructure; the standard highlights that misconfiguration here can block or degrade report access and functionality by breaking TLS negotiation between clients and backend components [Data: Entities (479, 486); Relationships (800, 802)]. The decision tree links TLS/cipher-suite incompatibility to observable symptoms such as report authors shown as “unknown,” indicating that TLS failures can manifest as metadata/authentication failures rather than obvious transport-level errors, and therefore must be included in runbooks and monitoring thresholds [Data: Relationships (804); Entities (491, 486)]. ## Service account connectivity and configuration are primary causes of missing author metadata The standard calls out service accounts as the identities used by reporting components to access the Data Mart and metadata; when report authors appear as “unknown,” the runbook specifically prescribes checking service account connectivity and permissions as a primary diagnostic action [Data: Entities (481, 491); Relationships (792, 805)]. This linkage makes service-account lifecycle controls (password/credential rotation, consistent configuration across environments) and monitoring of account authentication health operational priorities to avoid metadata loss and degraded author attribution [Data: Entities (481); Relationships (805)]. ## Decision tree maps symptoms to concrete component checks and environment-consistent change rules The document section containing decision trees and use cases maps specific symptoms to component checks (Data Mart reset, SQL connection, AOS service, IIS configuration, cipher-suite/TLS checks), providing an actionable troubleshooting workflow that ties observable issues to discrete remediation steps [Data: Entities (484); Relationships (784, 806)]. The standard further prescribes operational principles such as synchronizing cipher-suite changes across environments and performing AOS restarts after certain adjustments, which reduce the risk of asymmetric changes causing environment-specific failures; these procedural constraints should be enforced by change control and deployment automation [Data: Entities (476); Relationships (784)]. ## Recurring failure mode pattern: TLS/cipher mismatches and service-account authentication produce metadata anomalies The provided artifacts explicitly link TLS/cipher-suite incompatibility to both transport-level failures and the symptom of unknown report authors, and they separately identify service-account authentication problems as a cause of the same symptom, demonstrating a common failure-mode cluster where authentication/crypto misconfiguration and identity issues produce indistinct metadata anomalies [Data: Relationships (804, 805); Entities (486, 481, 491)]. Because multiple components (IIS/TLS layer, SQL backend/Data Mart, service accounts) can manifest the same operational symptom, the decision-tree's ordered checks (service accounts, TLS, SQL, Data Mart reset) should be codified into runbooks and automated diagnostics to avoid repeated firefighting and to direct engineers to the most-likely root cause quickly [Data: Entities (484, 478, 482); Relationships (792, 806, 795)]. ## Operational recovery guidance exists but relies on environment consistency and synchronous changes The standard contains explicit instructions that certain configuration changes (for example, allowed cipher suites) be synchronized across environments and that AOS restarts and Data Mart resets be used in a controlled manner; this indicates that successful recovery depends on disciplined change management and environment parity [Data: Entities (476, 486, 489); Relationships (784, 795)]. Absent automated enforcement of environment parity and pre-change compatibility checks, there is risk that asynchronous or partial rollouts (e.g., different cipher suites in UAT vs PROD) will create intermittent, environment-specific failures; the document's direction therefore implies the need for gating changes through the standard's decision-tree and CI/CD controls [Data: Entities (476, 486); Relationships (784)]. ## Controls and monitoring gaps implied by the document should be prioritized for remediation The artifact set highlights the criticality of TLS/cipher configuration, SQL/Data Mart health, and service-account authentication but does not itself include explicit monitoring/alert thresholds or automated remediation steps in the extracted records; given these components' operational impact, establishing targeted alerts for TLS negotiation failures, SQL connectivity errors, service-account authentication failures, and Data Mart consistency checks should be a near-term priority [Data: Entities (479, 482, 481, 478); Relationships (800, 806, 792, 795)]. Formalizing these checks into runbooks and integrating them with the decision-tree/use-cases in the standard (for example, automated pre-flight checks before cipher-suite changes and automated Data Mart consistency verification) will reduce time-to-detect and time-to-recover for the recurring failure modes documented here [Data: Entities (484, 476); Relationships (784)].

REPORT 2: # TRAIN CE / ADFS Integration: CE Train Server, ADFS Client Registration, and Toolbox MFA This community centers on the TRAIN CE/CE Train server integration that relies on on‑premises ADFS for authentication. A newly registered ADFS client used by the Toolbox to orchestrate MFA had EnableJWT and RequestMFAFromClaimsProviders enabled in PROD, which caused ADFS to issue JWTs and to forward MFA to external claims providers; those changes broke non‑browser integrations and produced widespread login/connectivity failures across environments. The incident affected the Train CE server, involved remediation actions (repair installation, federation metadata updates, certificate removals), and required coordinated troubleshooting by internal support engineers, the Identity/Architecture team and an external vendor (Avanade). Secondary factors observed during remediation included stale device compliance from hybrid Azure AD‑joined devices and Conditional Access evaluations that contributed to access variability for some users. The investigation produced both an ADFS configuration documentation artifact and guidance in a broader Identity & Authentication blueprint to prevent recurrence and to inform escalation and monitoring practices. ## Root cause: misconfigured ADFS client registration (EnableJWT + RequestMFAFromClaimsProviders) on a new client The investigation identified a new client application whose ADFS client registration had EnableJWT and RequestMFAFromClaimsProviders set to true; this caused ADFS to issue JWTs and to delegate MFA to external claims providers, which the downstream Toolbox/Train integration did not expect and therefore failed to authenticate. The new client registration and the parameter choices are explicitly recorded as the root cause and were adjusted to match the TEST environment to resolve the failures [Data: Entities (249,248,240,241); Relationships (390,370)]. Recommendation: enforce a documented client‑registration review process that verifies token format and MFA delegation flags before promoting client registrations to PROD, and add a checklist item to the CI/CD or change request to confirm EnableJWT and RequestMFAFromClaimsProviders settings for non‑browser integrations. ## Production impact and remediation steps applied to the CE Train server The CE Train server (TRAIN CE) experienced connectivity and authentication failures across users and environments; remediation steps recorded include a repair installation of the CE Train server (02.12.24), federation metadata updates on ADFS, and removal/rotation of old federation certificates—followed by ADFS parameter changes that restored correct behaviour. These actions and the target system are captured in the incident notes and ticket timeline [Data: Entities (252,235,254,253); Relationships (405,409,410,367)]. Recommendation: codify the validated remediation sequence (repair, federation metadata verification, certificate refresh, client parameter alignment) into a runbook for TRAIN CE integrations so responders can execute a repeatable playbook during future authentication incidents. ## Stakeholder and escalation map: support engineers, identity/architecture, application owners and external vendor involvement Multiple named stakeholders participated in triage and remediation: Employee 21 acted as lead analyst/owner for related incidents and executed configuration changes and device sync operations; Employee 14 reported and coordinated the initial ticket; Employee 24 and Admin appear in the ticket transfer chain; Avanade participated as an external troubleshooting partner; and the affected tenant and application owners are Customer Company 11AG / 11CE TRAIN. The ticket and assignment flows show cross‑team responsibility and vendor engagement during the incident [Data: Entities (232,4,231,10,229,230); Relationships (353,361,391,17,365)]. Recommendation: publish a concise stakeholder contact matrix and an escalation path (first responder/System Engineer -> Identity/Architecture -> Application Owner -> Vendor) and include roles, expected SLAs, and preferred communication channels for TRAIN CE‑class authentication incidents. ## Conditional Access and device sync were contributing factors for some access failures but were not the primary root cause Conditional Access was evaluated and did not present an outright blocking rule for the affected sign‑ins; however, some hybrid Azure AD‑joined devices reported stale or 'unknown' compliance for 6–8 hours, causing the 'Require compliant device' CA policy to evaluate as not satisfied and producing access variability for certain users. Employee 21 triggered manual device synchronization as part of remediation to update device compliance state faster [Data: Entities (683,680,688,684); Relationships (1199,1201,1206,1189)]. Recommendation: implement monitoring/alerts for device‑sync latency and include device synchronization health in incident triage dashboards; where appropriate, create temporary CA policy exceptions documented in change control but tied to a limited remediation window. ## Logging, monitoring and documentation gaps identified and addressed with configuration documentation and blueprint guidance The post‑incident artifacts include an ADFS configuration documentation entry capturing the corrected client registration settings and the investigation details, authored/confirmed by Employee 21, and a separate Identity & Authentication blueprint that prescribes decision trees, token format guidance, and escalation routes. The blueprint highlights the need for correlated logging across ADFS, claims providers and Entra ID to diagnose delegated MFA and JWT/SAML token flows [Data: Entities (244,232,207,219,208); Relationships (364,317,330)]. Recommendation: ensure the ADFS configuration documentation is versioned in the configuration management repository and update monitoring/alerting to collect token issuance events, claims‑provider logs and correlation IDs to shorten future diagnosis time. ## Token format and non‑browser flow mismatch risk (JWT vs SAML) for applications Enabling JWT issuance via EnableJWT changes the token format emitted by ADFS (JWT/OIDC) and requires that consuming applications support and validate JWTs; the blueprint warns that non‑browser integrations and service accounts commonly fail when token formats change unexpectedly. The incident demonstrates this risk where the new client caused JWT issuance that the Train integration could not process [Data: Entities (240,242,215,216); Relationships (379,324,334)]. Recommendation: require application owners to declare supported token formats in application inventories and gate any client registration change that switches token formats with explicit owner approval and a test plan validating non‑browser flows. ## Process and governance weakness: PROD‑only client registration change and insufficient pre‑deployment verification Investigators initially considered and briefly excluded ADFS because only PROD had been modified over the weekend; the error reproduced across environments, and only later was the misconfigured new client identified as the root cause—indicating a gap in change control or rollout validation for client registrations in PROD. The ticket timeline and decision notes record that the misconfigured registration was corrected to match TEST [Data: Entities (21,249,251); Relationships (65,228,390)]. Recommendation: implement formal change controls for ADFS client registrations (including peer review and a rollback plan), require a documented mapping between TEST and PROD registration parameters, and add pre‑promotion automated checks that detect risky flags like EnableJWT and RequestMFAFromClaimsProviders in non‑browser client contexts. ## Operationalizing the Decision Tree and runbook: where to escalate and what telemetry to collect The Identity & Authentication blueprint includes a decision tree for authentication issues (question flow for browser vs non‑browser, token type checks, MFA delegation checks and escalation criteria). context: SOURCE 40: ﻿general info & description Active Directory Domain Controller & Microsoft Defender for Identity Standard Version 1.0 Date: 10.10.2024 Status: Approved This document describes the architecture, security requirements and operational standards for Domain Controllers with installed Microsoft Defender for Identity Sensor within Customer Company 10. The objective is: • Stable monitoring of Active Directory activities • Proper functionality of the Defender for Identity sensors • Ensuring the necessary network and firewall permissions • Standardized configuration across all environments (On-Prem and Azure DCs) Microsoft Defender for Identity sensors run directly on the Domain Controllers and analyze: • Authentication processes • Kerberos and NTLM traffic • LDAP communication • Replication behavior Misconfigurations typically result in: • Sensor status “unhealthy” • Connection errors to the Defender Cloud Service • Missing security alerts • Recurring health alerts design Architecture overview Domain Controller (On-Prem / Azure) → Defender for Identity Sensor → Windows Firewall → Corporate Firewall → Microsoft Defender Cloud Service Components 1. Domain Controller Hosts Active Directory Services and the Defender Sensor 2. Defender for Identity Sensor Captures and analyzes authentication and network traffic 3. Firewall Layer o Windows Firewall on DC o Perimeter firewall o Azure NSG (for Azure DCs) 4. Defender Cloud Service Receives telemetry data for security analysis Network and port requirements Domain Controllers with sensor require: • Outbound HTTPS connection (TCP 443) • DNS resolution of external Microsoft endpoints • Access to specific Defender Service URLs • Internal AD ports (LDAP, Kerberos, RPC) unchanged Blocked or filtered connections result in “unhealthy” status. Operational principles • Sensor installation must be performed consistently on all DCs • Firewall rules must be configured according to Microsoft recommendations • Changes to network segments must be reviewed • After agent reinstallation, health status must be monitored • Parallel installation of multiple sensors per DC is not permitted decision tree & use cases Initial question Is only a single Domain Controller affected or multiple? A) Only one DC affected 1. Is the Defender Sensor service running? 2. Is outbound HTTPS connectivity available (Port 443)? 3. Is DNS resolution to Microsoft Defender endpoints successful? 4. Check event logs (Sensor / network errors) 5. If installation is inconsistent → remove agent and reinstall B) Multiple DCs affected 1. Has a firewall or NSG rule been changed? 2. Has a network segment been redefined? 3. Has a proxy or TLS policy been modified? 4. Is general internet connectivity available? C) Sensor repeatedly switches from “healthy” to “unhealthy” 1. Check for temporary network interruption 2. Check TLS or certificate issues 3. Check proxy authentication 4. Check agent version D) Issue persists after reinstallation 1. Verify that firewall rules are complete 2. Check whether Azure NSG blocks outbound connections 3. Check Defender Service Health Portal 4. Is internal security software blocking telemetry? REPORT 4: # KF10.com Domain Controllers and Microsoft Defender for Identity Sensors The community centers on Active Directory Domain Controllers (on‑prem and Azure) with Microsoft Defender for Identity sensors installed; sensors run on the DC hosts, send telemetry to the Defender Cloud Service over outbound HTTPS (TCP 443), and depend on a chain of network and host controls (Windows Firewall, perimeter firewall, Azure NSG), TLS/certificate validity, proxy configuration and local security software. The environment is governed by a written architecture/operational standard and a decision tree/troubleshooting flow that prescribes checks for service state, connectivity (DNS/443), event logs, TLS/proxy, and reinstall/monitor steps. Disruptions to any of these dependencies cause sensors to report “unhealthy”, reduce or eliminate health alerts in the cloud, and create blind spots in AD authentication/replication monitoring. [Data: Entities (696,697,698,702,699); Relationships (1231,1235,1255,1253,1258)] ## Domain Controllers and Defender sensors are tightly coupled and form the core monitoring plane Defender sensors run directly on Domain Controllers and capture authentication (Kerberos/NTLM), LDAP and replication telemetry; DC health and sensor behavior are tightly coupled, making the DCs and sensors a single operational surface that must be maintained together. The standard and operational guidance explicitly treat DCs with sensors as the primary subject for architecture and operations. This coupling means DC outages, host‑level changes, or misconfigured sensors directly degrade AD security visibility. [Data: Entities (696,697), Relationships (1231,1245,1246,1228)] ## Outbound HTTPS (TCP 443) to the Defender Cloud is a single critical network dependency Sensors rely on outbound HTTPS to send telemetry to the Defender Cloud Service; blocking or filtering TCP 443 prevents telemetry delivery and causes sensors to be marked unhealthy. The relationship between sensors and the cloud service is explicit and the document lists outbound HTTPS as a mandatory requirement. Network edge/firewall rules and Azure NSGs are singled out as controls that can interrupt this egress, which means perimeter and cloud egress policies are a primary root cause class for multi‑DC connectivity failures. [Data: Entities (702,698), Relationships (1235,1255,1258)] ## Host and perimeter network controls (Windows Firewall, perimeter firewall, Azure NSG) are frequent gating factors On‑host Windows Firewall settings on on‑prem DCs, perimeter firewall rules at the network edge, and Azure NSG rules for cloud DCs are all called out as required to permit sensor outbound connections and internal AD ports. Misconfiguration anywhere in this chain can simultaneously block telemetry and break AD protocols (LDAP/Kerberos/RPC), producing both service impact and monitoring blind spots. This indicates a need for coordinated change control across host, network, and cloud teams when modifying firewall/NSG rules. [Data: Entities (699,700,701,703), Relationships (1253,1255,1258,1261)] ## TLS/certificate problems, proxy authentication, and internal security software are persistent secondary failure modes The document lists TLS/certificate issues as an item that can prevent secure HTTPS between sensors and Microsoft endpoints; it also calls out proxy configuration/authentication and internal security software (host or inline appliances) as causes that can block or interfere with sensor egress. TLS mismatches or policy changes can cause failed egress despite port availability, while proxies or local security controls may require explicit exceptions or credentials for the sensors. These are common categories of intermittent or oscillating healthy/unhealthy sensor reports and should be included in root‑cause workflows. [Data: Entities (705,704,714), Relationships (1242,1241,1249,1260)] ## Sensor operational state, event logs and the decision tree are the primary diagnostic artefacts Operational checks emphasize verifying the sensor service state (running/reinstall), reviewing local sensor and system event logs, and following the decision tree that differentiates single‑DC vs multi‑DC issues. The decision tree maps to concrete troubleshooting steps: service state, outbound connectivity/DNS, proxy/TLS checks, logs and reinstall guidance; the Defender Service Health Portal is recommended to separate tenant/local problems from Microsoft service outages. These diagnostic artefacts should be centrally collected and accessible to on‑call teams for faster triage. [Data: Entities (708,712,709,706), Relationships (1243,1247,1264,1263)] ## Unhealthy sensors lead to missing or delayed health alerts and create security visibility gaps Sensor telemetry feeds the health/alerting pipeline in the Defender Cloud Service; when sensors are unhealthy or cannot reach the cloud, health alerts are absent or delayed, reducing detection capability for authentication anomalies and replication issues. This is a direct security and compliance exposure because AD authentication telemetry is critical for detecting lateral movement and credential misuse in the environment. The linkage between sensor connectivity and alert production is explicitly documented. [Data: Entities (713,698), Relationships (1248,1235)] ## Standards and the architecture document exist but network/topology change control is an elevated risk area An architecture and operational standard (Active Directory Domain Controller & Microsoft Defender for Identity Standard V1.0) and a decision tree/troubleshooting flow are documented, prescribing required ports and operational checks. However, the document specifically requires review of network segment/topology changes because such changes can alter the effective scope of perimeter firewall rules and NSGs, potentially blocking sensor traffic. This identifies network/topology change management as a governance gap to enforce when changes are planned. [Data: Entities (695,709,707), Relationships (1228,1256)] ## Priority remediation and operational recommendations align directly to documented checks Immediate high‑value actions are to: (1) verify outbound HTTPS (TCP 443) egress from affected DCs (host and perimeter/NSG), (2) confirm the sensor service is running and review sensor/system event logs, (3) validate TLS/certificate validity and proxy authentication/whitelisting, and (4) consult the Defender Service Health Portal to rule out cloud service issues. These steps are the same checks defined in the decision tree and should be codified into an escalation runbook and enforced via change control for firewall/NSG and topology changes to avoid recurrence. Implementing centralized log collection and automated health monitoring for sensor state would reduce MTTR. [Data: Entities (702,708,705,709), Relationships (1255,1243,1242,1263)]

This sequence is central to the incident's root-cause hypothesis and was specifically recorded in the log entries. [Data: Entities (537, 523); Relationships (890, 232, 883, 891)]. Contact Person 11 explicitly requested a rollback of the Windows version on the print servers, reinforcing the operational suspicion that the Windows update/version was implicated; the infrastructure on‑call also documented this suspicion. The appearance of BSODs after the restore further suggests OS/driver/host-level instability that must be investigated beyond application-level spooler troubleshooting. [Data: Relationships (923, 911); Entities (542)]. ## Failure manifested in both SAP printserver VMs but not in follow‑me print infrastructure Both VRINFRGERPRNT03 and VRINFRGERPRNT04 exhibited identical failure patterns (inability to process SAP jobs, failed spooler restarts, and BSODs after restore), while other print servers supporting the follow‑me solution continued to print normally. This scoped the incident to the SAP-designated printserver VMs rather than a tenant-wide network or SAP-side-only problem. [Data: Entities (154, 155, 535); Relationships (913, 901, 887)]. Because two independent VMs in the same role failed similarly after the same maintenance/change sequence, a systemic image, driver set, or host-level change (or a common post-restore validation omission) is a likely common-mode cause that requires coordinated forensic analysis across both VM instances and their hypervisor/backup/restore processes. [Data: Relationships (890, 232)]. ## Operational runbook/validation gaps: post-update/restore checks were insufficient or incomplete An approved 'Print Services for SAP Output' standard exists and mandates post-update/restore validation steps (queue status, spooler health, publishing/sharing parameters and test prints). The incident narrative cites VM restore and Windows update as triggers requiring these checks, but the persistent failure and need to manually toggle network shares indicate either incomplete adherence to the standard or that the current validation checklist does not detect the specific failure mode observed. [Data: Entities (147, 161); Relationships (243, 246, 251)]. This demonstrates both the presence of governance (a standard) and a procedural gap: either the runbook was not fully executed during the restoration window or the runbook lacks items (e.g., explicit verification of network-share registration and directory publishing state after restore) that would have detected this issue before business impact. The standard should be revised to explicitly include steps that caught this failure in TI-931293. [Data: Relationships (251, 891)]. ## Troubleshooting sequence and stakeholder actions were appropriate but reveal handover and evidence-capture weaknesses Employee 23 performed early remediation actions (gpupdate /force on an affected workstation, spooler restart attempts, and remote troubleshooting) and then activated infrastructure on‑call; the infrastructure on‑call performed the VM restore with support from a technician (Mister X) and restoration contexts (Inter12203/Inter12204). Multiple engineers later investigated BSODs and log evidence. These documented steps show correct escalation and involvement of required roles. [Data: Entities (49, 538, 541, 539, 540); Relationships (74, 921, 908, 924, 925)]. However, the fact that BSODs and the network-share corruption occurred after the restore suggests that pre‑ and post-restore evidence capture (detailed event/log snapshots, hypervisor logs, driver versions) and controlled validation were either not available or not sufficient to prevent or immediately diagnose the failure — an evidence-capture checklist and mandatory pre-remediation snapshot of key logs should be formalised. [Data: Relationships (911, 890)]. ## Security and availability exposures from share toggling and manual fixes Administrators cleared stuck jobs and restored printing by disabling and then re-enabling the printer network shares on the affected servers. While effective in this incident, this manual share manipulation is an operationally risky fix if done without change control because it temporarily alters SMB/print sharing state and could impact visibility or cause job loss if not coordinated and audited. The logs show that the printer network share was the primary locus of the problem and the point of manual remediation. [Data: Relationships (883, 891); Entities (524)]. Recommendation: treat share toggles as controlled mitigation steps in runbooks with explicit rollback/validation and audit logging to prevent accidental exposure or data loss. Consider automating share/publishing verification as part of post-update validation to avoid manual toggles except under controlled emergency procedures. [Data: Relationships (251, 246)]. ## Recommended immediate and medium-term actions (prioritised) Immediate (P0/P1) actions: 1) Isolate a restored printserver VM to collect memory dumps, Windows event logs, spooler logs and hypervisor host logs for the restore window to determine BSOD causes; 2) if not already done, roll back to the previously known-good Windows image/version on a non-production test copy to reproduce the failure before applying tenant-wide rollbacks; 3) add mandatory post-restore checklist enforcement (validate queue properties, publishing/sharing flags, directory publishing and run an SAP-originated test print) before returning servers to production. These map to items recorded in the standard and the ticket actions. [Data: Entities (147, 523, 537); Relationships (243, 246, 923)]. Medium-term actions: 1) Augment the 'Print Services for SAP Output' standard with explicit post-restore checks for network-share registration and add automated probes that verify queue visibility from SAP and a test workstation; 2) implement a pre/post-restore automated verification job that runs spooler/queue/publishing checks and preserves logs for forensics; 3) perform a controlled update/rollback plan for Windows versions across SAP printserver VMs (staged on a test VM, with clear rollback criteria). These changes will reduce recurrence and shorten mean time to resolution for similar future incidents. [Data: Entities (147, 161, 523); Relationships (890, 891)]. ## Communication, ownership and escalation matrix to formalise The incident involved CRM/web ticket logging, a primary early responder (Employee 23), escalation to infrastructure on‑call, handover to early shift for validation, and involvement of additional engineers (Employees 19 and 20) for BSOD and SAP-side checks. This shows a functioning ad-hoc escalation chain but also highlights the need for a formal matrix documenting responsibilities for: immediate remediation (spooler/queue checks), VM restore/host interactions, OS-level diagnostics, SAP-side validation, and customer communication/approvals. [Data: Entities (49, 516, 517, 538, 543); Relationships (74, 921, 905, 879, 906)]. Create an escalation runbook that lists required evidence to collect at each handover (logs, timestamps, snapshots), decision criteria for rollback vs. rebuild, and a named on-call roster to reduce ambiguity in future incidents. Ensure the runbook maps to the approved standard and is practiced in tabletop exercises. [Data: Entities (147); Relationships (905)].

In the new client application used specifically for the MFA request of the Toolbox on the Train system, the following parameters were set to true: EnableJWT RequestMFAFromClaimsProviders EnableJWT activates the issuance of JSON Web Tokens in the authentication process. If enabled, ADFS issues JWTs instead of other token formats such as SAML. These tokens are evaluated by the target application. If the target system does not support JWTs or cannot validate their signature, authentication fails. RequestMFAFromClaimsProviders forwards MFA requirements to configured claims providers. This delegates MFA execution to external providers and increases complexity in authentication flow, logging, and auditing. The combination of these parameters can lead to login failures if the target system is not designed to handle JWTs or external MFA claims. We have documented the configuration to avoid future issues. From our perspective, no further problems are expected after adjusting the parameters. If MFA works stably and the Train environment is reachable again, the login topic can be considered resolved. context: SOURCE 40: ﻿general info & description Active Directory Domain Controller & Microsoft Defender for Identity Standard Version 1.0 Date: 10.10.2024 Status: Approved This document describes the architecture, security requirements and operational standards for Domain Controllers with installed Microsoft Defender for Identity Sensor within Customer Company 10. The objective is: • Stable monitoring of Active Directory activities • Proper functionality of the Defender for Identity sensors • Ensuring the necessary network and firewall permissions • Standardized configuration across all environments (On-Prem and Azure DCs) Microsoft Defender for Identity sensors run directly on the Domain Controllers and analyze: • Authentication processes • Kerberos and NTLM traffic • LDAP communication • Replication behavior Misconfigurations typically result in: • Sensor status “unhealthy” • Connection errors to the Defender Cloud Service • Missing security alerts • Recurring health alerts design Architecture overview Domain Controller (On-Prem / Azure) → Defender for Identity Sensor → Windows Firewall → Corporate Firewall → Microsoft Defender Cloud Service Components 1. Domain Controller Hosts Active Directory Services and the Defender Sensor 2. Defender for Identity Sensor Captures and analyzes authentication and network traffic 3. Firewall Layer o Windows Firewall on DC o Perimeter firewall o Azure NSG (for Azure DCs) 4. Defender Cloud Service Receives telemetry data for security analysis Network and port requirements Domain Controllers with sensor require: • Outbound HTTPS connection (TCP 443) • DNS resolution of external Microsoft endpoints • Access to specific Defender Service URLs • Internal AD ports (LDAP, Kerberos, RPC) unchanged Blocked or filtered connections result in “unhealthy” status. Operational principles • Sensor installation must be performed consistently on all DCs • Firewall rules must be configured according to Microsoft recommendations • Changes to network segments must be reviewed • After agent reinstallation, health status must be monitored • Parallel installation of multiple sensors per DC is not permitted decision tree & use cases Initial question Is only a single Domain Controller affected or multiple? A) Only one DC affected 1. Is the Defender Sensor service running? 2. Is outbound HTTPS connectivity available (Port 443)? 3. Is DNS resolution to Microsoft Defender endpoints successful? 4. Check event logs (Sensor / network errors) 5. If installation is inconsistent → remove agent and reinstall B) Multiple DCs affected 1. Has a firewall or NSG rule been changed? 2. Has a network segment been redefined? 3. Has a proxy or TLS policy been modified? 4. Is general internet connectivity available? C) Sensor repeatedly switches from “healthy” to “unhealthy” 1. Check for temporary network interruption 2. Check TLS or certificate issues 3. Check proxy authentication 4. Check agent version D) Issue persists after reinstallation 1. Verify that firewall rules are complete 2. Check whether Azure NSG blocks outbound connections 3. Check Defender Service Health Portal 4. Is internal security software blocking telemetry? REPORT 4: # KF10.com Domain Controllers and Microsoft Defender for Identity Sensors The community centers on Active Directory Domain Controllers (on‑prem and Azure) with Microsoft Defender for Identity sensors installed; sensors run on the DC hosts, send telemetry to the Defender Cloud Service over outbound HTTPS (TCP 443), and depend on a chain of network and host controls (Windows Firewall, perimeter firewall, Azure NSG), TLS/certificate validity, proxy configuration and local security software. The environment is governed by a written architecture/operational standard and a decision tree/troubleshooting flow that prescribes checks for service state, connectivity (DNS/443), event logs, TLS/proxy, and reinstall/monitor steps. Disruptions to any of these dependencies cause sensors to report “unhealthy”, reduce or eliminate health alerts in the cloud, and create blind spots in AD authentication/replication monitoring. [Data: Entities (696,697,698,702,699); Relationships (1231,1235,1255,1253,1258)] ## Domain Controllers and Defender sensors are tightly coupled and form the core monitoring plane Defender sensors run directly on Domain Controllers and capture authentication (Kerberos/NTLM), LDAP and replication telemetry; DC health and sensor behavior are tightly coupled, making the DCs and sensors a single operational surface that must be maintained together. The standard and operational guidance explicitly treat DCs with sensors as the primary subject for architecture and operations. This coupling means DC outages, host‑level changes, or misconfigured sensors directly degrade AD security visibility. [Data: Entities (696,697), Relationships (1231,1245,1246,1228)] ## Outbound HTTPS (TCP 443) to the Defender Cloud is a single critical network dependency Sensors rely on outbound HTTPS to send telemetry to the Defender Cloud Service; blocking or filtering TCP 443 prevents telemetry delivery and causes sensors to be marked unhealthy. The relationship between sensors and the cloud service is explicit and the document lists outbound HTTPS as a mandatory requirement. Network edge/firewall rules and Azure NSGs are singled out as controls that can interrupt this egress, which means perimeter and cloud egress policies are a primary root cause class for multi‑DC connectivity failures. [Data: Entities (702,698), Relationships (1235,1255,1258)] ## Host and perimeter network controls (Windows Firewall, perimeter firewall, Azure NSG) are frequent gating factors On‑host Windows Firewall settings on on‑prem DCs, perimeter firewall rules at the network edge, and Azure NSG rules for cloud DCs are all called out as required to permit sensor outbound connections and internal AD ports. Misconfiguration anywhere in this chain can simultaneously block telemetry and break AD protocols (LDAP/Kerberos/RPC), producing both service impact and monitoring blind spots. This indicates a need for coordinated change control across host, network, and cloud teams when modifying firewall/NSG rules. [Data: Entities (699,700,701,703), Relationships (1253,1255,1258,1261)] ## TLS/certificate problems, proxy authentication, and internal security software are persistent secondary failure modes The document lists TLS/certificate issues as an item that can prevent secure HTTPS between sensors and Microsoft endpoints; it also calls out proxy configuration/authentication and internal security software (host or inline appliances) as causes that can block or interfere with sensor egress. TLS mismatches or policy changes can cause failed egress despite port availability, while proxies or local security controls may require explicit exceptions or credentials for the sensors. These are common categories of intermittent or oscillating healthy/unhealthy sensor reports and should be included in root‑cause workflows. [Data: Entities (705,704,714), Relationships (1242,1241,1249,1260)] ## Sensor operational state, event logs and the decision tree are the primary diagnostic artefacts Operational checks emphasize verifying the sensor service state (running/reinstall), reviewing local sensor and system event logs, and following the decision tree that differentiates single‑DC vs multi‑DC issues. The decision tree maps to concrete troubleshooting steps: service state, outbound connectivity/DNS, proxy/TLS checks, logs and reinstall guidance; the Defender Service Health Portal is recommended to separate tenant/local problems from Microsoft service outages. These diagnostic artefacts should be centrally collected and accessible to on‑call teams for faster triage. [Data: Entities (708,712,709,706), Relationships (1243,1247,1264,1263)] ## Unhealthy sensors lead to missing or delayed health alerts and create security visibility gaps Sensor telemetry feeds the health/alerting pipeline in the Defender Cloud Service; when sensors are unhealthy or cannot reach the cloud, health alerts are absent or delayed, reducing detection capability for authentication anomalies and replication issues. This is a direct security and compliance exposure because AD authentication telemetry is critical for detecting lateral movement and credential misuse in the environment. The linkage between sensor connectivity and alert production is explicitly documented. [Data: Entities (713,698), Relationships (1248,1235)] ## Standards and the architecture document exist but network/topology change control is an elevated risk area An architecture and operational standard (Active Directory Domain Controller & Microsoft Defender for Identity Standard V1.0) and a decision tree/troubleshooting flow are documented, prescribing required ports and operational checks. However, the document specifically requires review of network segment/topology changes because such changes can alter the effective scope of perimeter firewall rules and NSGs, potentially blocking sensor traffic. This identifies network/topology change management as a governance gap to enforce when changes are planned. [Data: Entities (695,709,707), Relationships (1228,1256)] ## Priority remediation and operational recommendations align directly to documented checks Immediate high‑value actions are to: (1) verify outbound HTTPS (TCP 443) egress from affected DCs (host and perimeter/NSG), (2) confirm the sensor service is running and review sensor/system event logs, (3) validate TLS/certificate validity and proxy authentication/whitelisting, and (4) consult the Defender Service Health Portal to rule out cloud service issues. These steps are the same checks defined in the decision tree and should be codified into an escalation runbook and enforced via change control for firewall/NSG and topology changes to avoid recurrence. Implementing centralized log collection and automated health monitoring for sensor state would reduce MTTR. [Data: Entities (702,708,705,709), Relationships (1255,1243,1242,1263)]

REPORT 16: # SAP Printing Outage — VRINFRGERPRNT03/04 (TI-931293) The community centers on the SAP printing path where SAP Spool/Output Management delivers jobs to SAP Print Targets that map to dedicated Windows Printserver VMs (notably VRINFRGERPRNT03 and VRINFRGERPRNT04). A high‑priority production incident (TI-931293) occurred 17–19.07.24 when SAP-generated orders could not be printed, blocking deliveries; the immediate remediation sequence included spooler restarts, a VM restore to a pre-maintenance snapshot, and disabling/re-enabling printer network shares which ultimately cleared queued jobs. The incident produced blue screens (BSOD) on the restored VMs and a customer request to rollback Windows versioning, and it exposed gaps in post-update/restore validation for queue properties, publishing/sharing parameters and end‑to‑end test prints. Multiple support engineers and the infrastructure on‑call team coordinated the recovery; the organisation has an SAP printing standard that prescribes post-change validation but the event still recurred across both SAP printserver VMs, indicating a likely server-image or Windows-update related root cause that requires deeper OS/driver/VM-host investigation and tighter runbook controls. [Data: Entities (534, 153, 154, 155, 147); Relationships (896, 913, 908, 232)] ## Production-impacting outage: SAP print jobs blocked, business deliveries affected The incident was recorded as a high‑priority printing outage affecting SAP-originated orders and deliveries, indicating direct business impact and the need for fast remediation and coordinated operations response. The ticket TI-931293 documents the inability to print from SAP and the classification and remediation timeline across 17–19.07.24. [Data: Entities (534); Relationships (896)]. Support actions were escalated to infrastructure on‑call and multiple engineers were assigned to the case, showing cross-shift coordination and handover requirements for production restores and follow-up testing (information sent to early shift for next‑day validation). This highlights both the operational severity and that the incident required multi-role participation to resolve. [Data: Relationships (905, 908)]. ## Probable root‑cause chain: Windows update -> VM restore -> network share/queue publishing corruption Documents and timelines link a Windows update and subsequent VM restore to the onset of queue-sharing and printing failures: a Windows update was applied, the print-server VMs were restored to a prior snapshot, and after the restore the printer network shares and queue publishing were malfunctioning, causing jobs to remain stuck until shares were toggled. This sequence is central to the incident's root-cause hypothesis and was specifically recorded in the log entries. [Data: Entities (537, 523); Relationships (890, 232, 883, 891)]. Contact Person 11 explicitly requested a rollback of the Windows version on the print servers, reinforcing the operational suspicion that the Windows update/version was implicated; the infrastructure on‑call also documented this suspicion. The appearance of BSODs after the restore further suggests OS/driver/host-level instability that must be investigated beyond application-level spooler troubleshooting. [Data: Relationships (923, 911); Entities (542)]. ## Failure manifested in both SAP printserver VMs but not in follow‑me print infrastructure Both VRINFRGERPRNT03 and VRINFRGERPRNT04 exhibited identical failure patterns (inability to process SAP jobs, failed spooler restarts, and BSODs after restore), while other print servers supporting the follow‑me solution continued to print normally. This scoped the incident to the SAP-designated printserver VMs rather than a tenant-wide network or SAP-side-only problem. [Data: Entities (154, 155, 535); Relationships (913, 901, 887)]. Because two independent VMs in the same role failed similarly after the same maintenance/change sequence, a systemic image, driver set, or host-level change (or a common post-restore validation omission) is a likely common-mode cause that requires coordinated forensic analysis across both VM instances and their hypervisor/backup/restore processes. [Data: Relationships (890, 232)]. ## Operational runbook/validation gaps: post-update/restore checks were insufficient or incomplete An approved 'Print Services for SAP Output' standard exists and mandates post-update/restore validation steps (queue status, spooler health, publishing/sharing parameters and test prints). The incident narrative cites VM restore and Windows update as triggers requiring these checks, but the persistent failure and need to manually toggle network shares indicate either incomplete adherence to the standard or that the current validation checklist does not detect the specific failure mode observed. [Data: Entities (147, 161); Relationships (243, 246, 251)]. This demonstrates both the presence of governance (a standard) and a procedural gap: either the runbook was not fully executed during the restoration window or the runbook lacks items (e.g., explicit verification of network-share registration and directory publishing state after restore) that would have detected this issue before business impact. The standard should be revised to explicitly include steps that caught this failure in TI-931293. [Data: Relationships (251, 891)]. ## Troubleshooting sequence and stakeholder actions were appropriate but reveal handover and evidence-capture weaknesses Employee 23 performed early remediation actions (gpupdate /force on an affected workstation, spooler restart attempts, and remote troubleshooting) and then activated infrastructure on‑call; the infrastructure on‑call performed the VM restore with support from a technician (Mister X) and restoration contexts (Inter12203/Inter12204). Multiple engineers later investigated BSODs and log evidence. These documented steps show correct escalation and involvement of required roles. [Data: Entities (49, 538, 541, 539, 540); Relationships (74, 921, 908, 924, 925)]. However, the fact that BSODs and the network-share corruption occurred after the restore suggests that pre‑ and post-restore evidence capture (detailed event/log snapshots, hypervisor logs, driver versions) and controlled validation were either not available or not sufficient to prevent or immediately diagnose the failure — an evidence-capture checklist and mandatory pre-remediation snapshot of key logs should be formalised. [Data: Relationships (911, 890)]. ## Security and availability exposures from share toggling and manual fixes Administrators cleared stuck jobs and restored printing by disabling and then re-enabling the printer network shares on the affected servers. While effective in this incident, this manual share manipulation is an operationally risky fix if done without change control because it temporarily alters SMB/print sharing state and could impact visibility or cause job loss if not coordinated and audited. The logs show that the printer network share was the primary locus of the problem and the point of manual remediation. [Data: Relationships (883, 891); Entities (524)]. Recommendation: treat share toggles as controlled mitigation steps in runbooks with explicit rollback/validation and audit logging to prevent accidental exposure or data loss. Consider automating share/publishing verification as part of post-update validation to avoid manual toggles except under controlled emergency procedures. [Data: Relationships (251, 246)]. ## Recommended immediate and medium-term actions (prioritised) Immediate (P0/P1) actions: 1) Isolate a restored printserver VM to collect memory dumps, Windows event logs, spooler logs and hypervisor host logs for the restore window to determine BSOD causes; 2) if not already done, roll back to the previously known-good Windows image/version on a non-production test copy to reproduce the failure before applying tenant-wide rollbacks; 3) add mandatory post-restore checklist enforcement (validate queue properties, publishing/sharing flags, directory publishing and run an SAP-originated test print) before returning servers to production. These map to items recorded in the standard and the ticket actions. [Data: Entities (147, 523, 537); Relationships (243, 246, 923)]. Medium-term actions: 1) Augment the 'Print Services for SAP Output' standard with explicit post-restore checks for network-share registration and add automated probes that verify queue visibility from SAP and a test workstation; 2) implement a pre/post-restore automated verification job that runs spooler/queue/publishing checks and preserves logs for forensics; 3) perform a controlled update/rollback plan for Windows versions across SAP printserver VMs (staged on a test VM, with clear rollback criteria). These changes will reduce recurrence and shorten mean time to resolution for similar future incidents. [Data: Entities (147, 161, 523); Relationships (890, 891)]. ## Communication, ownership and escalation matrix to formalise The incident involved CRM/web ticket logging, a primary early responder (Employee 23), escalation to infrastructure on‑call, handover to early shift for validation, and involvement of additional engineers (Employees 19 and 20) for BSOD and SAP-side checks. This shows a functioning ad-hoc escalation chain but also highlights the need for a formal matrix documenting responsibilities for: immediate remediation (spooler/queue checks), VM restore/host interactions, OS-level diagnostics, SAP-side validation, and customer communication/approvals. [Data: Entities (49, 516, 517, 538, 543); Relationships (74, 921, 905, 879, 906)]. Create an escalation runbook that lists required evidence to collect at each handover (logs, timestamps, snapshots), decision criteria for rollback vs. rebuild, and a named on-call roster to reduce ambiguity in future incidents. Ensure the runbook maps to the approved standard and is practiced in tabletop exercises. [Data: Entities (147); Relationships (905)].

In the new client application used specifically for the MFA request of the Toolbox on the Train system, the following parameters were set to true: EnableJWT RequestMFAFromClaimsProviders EnableJWT activates the issuance of JSON Web Tokens in the authentication process. If enabled, ADFS issues JWTs instead of other token formats such as SAML. These tokens are evaluated by the target application. If the target system does not support JWTs or cannot validate their signature, authentication fails. RequestMFAFromClaimsProviders forwards MFA requirements to configured claims providers. This delegates MFA execution to external providers and increases complexity in authentication flow, logging, and auditing. The combination of these parameters can lead to login failures if the target system is not designed to handle JWTs or external MFA claims. We have documented the configuration to avoid future issues. From our perspective, no further problems are expected after adjusting the parameters. If MFA works stably and the Train environment is reachable again, the login topic can be considered resolved. question: Is the statement supported by the evidence?
answer: . General knowledge note - Common patterns: TLS inspection or cipher/profile changes on a perimeter device can strip or alter headers and break backend assumptions, producing 502s; ADFS/federation metadata or certificate rotations can break token issuance and cause cross service authentication failures; OS patch/restore can break spooler/driver state and cause ERP printing to fail. These are well documented operational failure modes.

REPORT 4: # KF10.com Domain Controllers and Microsoft Defender for Identity Sensors The community centers on Active Directory Domain Controllers (on‑prem and Azure) with Microsoft Defender for Identity sensors installed; sensors run on the DC hosts, send telemetry to the Defender Cloud Service over outbound HTTPS (TCP 443), and depend on a chain of network and host controls (Windows Firewall, perimeter firewall, Azure NSG), TLS/certificate validity, proxy configuration and local security software. The environment is governed by a written architecture/operational standard and a decision tree/troubleshooting flow that prescribes checks for service state, connectivity (DNS/443), event logs, TLS/proxy, and reinstall/monitor steps. Disruptions to any of these dependencies cause sensors to report “unhealthy”, reduce or eliminate health alerts in the cloud, and create blind spots in AD authentication/replication monitoring. [Data: Entities (696,697,698,702,699); Relationships (1231,1235,1255,1253,1258)] ## Domain Controllers and Defender sensors are tightly coupled and form the core monitoring plane Defender sensors run directly on Domain Controllers and capture authentication (Kerberos/NTLM), LDAP and replication telemetry; DC health and sensor behavior are tightly coupled, making the DCs and sensors a single operational surface that must be maintained together. The standard and operational guidance explicitly treat DCs with sensors as the primary subject for architecture and operations. This coupling means DC outages, host‑level changes, or misconfigured sensors directly degrade AD security visibility. [Data: Entities (696,697), Relationships (1231,1245,1246,1228)] ## Outbound HTTPS (TCP 443) to the Defender Cloud is a single critical network dependency Sensors rely on outbound HTTPS to send telemetry to the Defender Cloud Service; blocking or filtering TCP 443 prevents telemetry delivery and causes sensors to be marked unhealthy. The relationship between sensors and the cloud service is explicit and the document lists outbound HTTPS as a mandatory requirement. Network edge/firewall rules and Azure NSGs are singled out as controls that can interrupt this egress, which means perimeter and cloud egress policies are a primary root cause class for multi‑DC connectivity failures. [Data: Entities (702,698), Relationships (1235,1255,1258)] ## Host and perimeter network controls (Windows Firewall, perimeter firewall, Azure NSG) are frequent gating factors On‑host Windows Firewall settings on on‑prem DCs, perimeter firewall rules at the network edge, and Azure NSG rules for cloud DCs are all called out as required to permit sensor outbound connections and internal AD ports. Misconfiguration anywhere in this chain can simultaneously block telemetry and break AD protocols (LDAP/Kerberos/RPC), producing both service impact and monitoring blind spots. This indicates a need for coordinated change control across host, network, and cloud teams when modifying firewall/NSG rules. [Data: Entities (699,700,701,703), Relationships (1253,1255,1258,1261)] ## TLS/certificate problems, proxy authentication, and internal security software are persistent secondary failure modes The document lists TLS/certificate issues as an item that can prevent secure HTTPS between sensors and Microsoft endpoints; it also calls out proxy configuration/authentication and internal security software (host or inline appliances) as causes that can block or interfere with sensor egress. TLS mismatches or policy changes can cause failed egress despite port availability, while proxies or local security controls may require explicit exceptions or credentials for the sensors. These are common categories of intermittent or oscillating healthy/unhealthy sensor reports and should be included in root‑cause workflows. [Data: Entities (705,704,714), Relationships (1242,1241,1249,1260)] ## Sensor operational state, event logs and the decision tree are the primary diagnostic artefacts Operational checks emphasize verifying the sensor service state (running/reinstall), reviewing local sensor and system event logs, and following the decision tree that differentiates single‑DC vs multi‑DC issues. The decision tree maps to concrete troubleshooting steps: service state, outbound connectivity/DNS, proxy/TLS checks, logs and reinstall guidance; the Defender Service Health Portal is recommended to separate tenant/local problems from Microsoft service outages. These diagnostic artefacts should be centrally collected and accessible to on‑call teams for faster triage. [Data: Entities (708,712,709,706), Relationships (1243,1247,1264,1263)] ## Unhealthy sensors lead to missing or delayed health alerts and create security visibility gaps Sensor telemetry feeds the health/alerting pipeline in the Defender Cloud Service; when sensors are unhealthy or cannot reach the cloud, health alerts are absent or delayed, reducing detection capability for authentication anomalies and replication issues. This is a direct security and compliance exposure because AD authentication telemetry is critical for detecting lateral movement and credential misuse in the environment. The linkage between sensor connectivity and alert production is explicitly documented. [Data: Entities (713,698), Relationships (1248,1235)] ## Standards and the architecture document exist but network/topology change control is an elevated risk area An architecture and operational standard (Active Directory Domain Controller & Microsoft Defender for Identity Standard V1.0) and a decision tree/troubleshooting flow are documented, prescribing required ports and operational checks. However, the document specifically requires review of network segment/topology changes because such changes can alter the effective scope of perimeter firewall rules and NSGs, potentially blocking sensor traffic.
